set.analysis_note: Sin is depicted as a predatory force crouching at the door of Cain's will, actively desiring to master him; the inner person must exert ruling agency to resist it.
Cain experiences his iniquity's consequence as an unbearable weight—punishment so overwhelming it exceeds his inner capacity to endure it.
The men of Sodom are identified as habitual, extreme sinners whose wrongdoing is directed against the Lord himself, marking a deep moral disposition of rebellion.
The Amorites' iniquity is presented as a measured moral accumulation not yet full; God withholds judgment until the crime-count reaches its complete measure.
Sodom's sin is characterized as exceedingly grave, its moral weight so great it has risen as a loud outcry before the Lord, prompting divine attention and impending judgment.
The punishment belonging to the city is so total that innocent Lot risks being swept away in it; the iniquity's consequence falls destructively on all within reach.
God affirms Abimelech acted from integrity of heart and intervened externally to prevent him from sinning, showing that the inner will alone was insufficient protection without divine restraint.
Abimelech protests he had not sinned against Abraham, yet Abraham's deception brought a great sin upon him; sinning here is an act that damages another person and their household.
Abraham's deception placed a great sin upon Abimelech's household—sin here is a moral burden transferred by another's wrongful act, causing communal harm.
Jacob challenges Laban to name any actual sin or offense that would justify his aggressive pursuit; sin here is a specific wrongdoing requiring evidence before it can rightly motivate action.
Jacob demands to know what transgression—what breach or rebellion—he has committed that justified Laban's heated pursuit; transgression here requires a concrete act to be charged.
Jacob did not bring losses from torn animals to Laban but bore them personally; the term implies the fault and financial liability for loss, here voluntarily assumed by Jacob himself.
Joseph refuses the temptation by naming it as sinning against God; his conscience recognizes that the action would be a moral violation directed against God, not merely a social offense.
The cupbearer and baker committed an offense against their lord the king; the act is directed against a specific authority, framing sin here as a breach of loyalty toward a superior.
The cupbearer recalls his offenses to Pharaoh—the term refers to a remembered failure of duty that now resurfaces in his conscience at a moment of moral relevance.
The brothers recognize inner guilt as the cause of their present distress; their conscience connects their past cruelty—ignoring their brother's soul-anguish—directly to their current suffering.
Reuben had warned against sinning against the boy; the act of harming Joseph is here named as a sin that now brings a required reckoning, linking the inner moral wrong to its just consequence.
Judah accepts personal accountability for Benjamin's safety; bearing the blame means the fault and its penalty would fall entirely on him, making the inner responsibility legally binding.
Judah confesses that God has uncovered the guilt of the servants; guilt here is a hidden moral reality that God exposes, rendering self-justification impossible before a just judge.
Judah pledged his life for Benjamin's safe return; bearing the blame before his father forever means he accepted permanent personal culpability should he fail.

The brothers' sin against Joseph is named alongside their transgression as something requiring explicit forgiveness; sin here is a moral wrong done to a person that creates a relational debt needing release.

The brothers' transgression against Joseph is an act of deliberate wrongdoing toward him, here framed as requiring forgiveness—Joseph's pardon is the only resolution for the relational rupture caused.
The taskmasters' abuse is called a fault lying within Pharaoh's own people; the term identifies culpable wrongdoing assigned to a party responsible for an unjust situation.

Pharaoh confesses 'I have sinned' in response to the plague, acknowledging the Lord is right and he himself wrong; the admission is externally prompted but formally names an inner moral failure against God.
Pharaoh sinned again by hardening his heart once the plague ceased; the act of sinning is explicitly linked to the will's hardening, showing the inner disposition driving repeated transgression.
Pharaoh confesses sinning against both the Lord and Moses; the sin is a relational offense directed at both divine and human parties, acknowledged under pressure of judgment.

Pharaoh asks forgiveness for his sin just this once, framing sin as something that must be pardoned before the resulting death-plague can be removed—sin and its lethal consequence are directly linked.

Idolatry is the iniquity God visits on subsequent generations; the crime of the fathers against God through idolatrous hatred carries consequences that extend across family lines as a moral inheritance.
God's terrifying presence is meant to implant fear in the inner person so that the will is restrained from sinning; the fear of God before one functions as an inner deterrent to moral failure.

The master who refuses to allow redemption and would sell the designated woman to foreigners has broken faith with her; treachery here is a betrayal of a personal covenant commitment that dishonors the other party.
Every breach of trust in disputed property cases constitutes transgression; the term names faithless violation of a custodial obligation, and God himself adjudicates who bears the guilt.
A bribe perverts the cause of righteous people by distorting the judge's perception; the act corrupts the inner faculty of discernment so that clear sight is lost and justice is twisted.
Rebelling against the angel constitutes transgression that God will not pardon; the unpardonable nature of this specific transgression is grounded in the angel bearing God's own name.
Allowing foreign nations to dwell in the land creates the danger that they will cause Israel to sin against God; the mechanism is entrapment through idolatrous influence acting on the will.
Aaron bears the guilt arising from Israel's holy offerings on his forehead before God; the guilt of ritual failure is transferred to the high priest's person so that the people remain accepted.

Priests who enter the sanctuary or approach the altar improperly bear guilt and face death; the iniquity is incurred when the inner boundary of holiness is violated through irregular priestly conduct.
A bull offered as a sin offering purifies the altar; the ritual act addresses moral or cultic contamination so that the sacred space is cleansed and consecrated for acceptable worship.
The daily sin offering is offered explicitly for atonement; atonement here is the purpose of the sacrifice, covering the defilement that prevents the altar from functioning as a holy meeting point.

Annual atonement made with sin-offering blood on the altar's horns consecrates it as most holy; the once-a-year rite covers accumulated impurity so the altar remains a valid place of divine encounter.

The atonement money given for the tent of meeting brings Israel to remembrance before God and makes atonement for their lives; ransom payment functions as the means of covering the moral debt that separates the people from God.
Moses confronts Aaron for bringing a great sin upon the people; the sin is a collective moral weight loaded onto the community by Aaron's leadership failure in permitting idolatry.

Moses declares the people have sinned a great sin and announces he will seek to make atonement for it; the gravity of the sin against God demands an intercessory act to restore the broken relationship.
The great sin is something Moses hopes to atone for before God; sin here is an offense requiring specific remedial action to repair the ruptured covenant between Israel and the Lord.
Moses declares the people have sinned greatly and announces an attempt at atonement; the sin is framed as a severe moral wrong against God requiring priestly intercession to potentially repair.
Moses confesses before God that the people have sinned a great sin by making gods of gold; the inner nature of the sin is idolatrous self-will that supplants God with a human-made substitute.
Moses names the making of golden gods a great sin as he intercedes before the Lord; sin here is the specific act of idolatry, named plainly as the moral content of what the people have done.

Moses pleads for the people's sin to be forgiven, and offers to be blotted from God's book if not; the sin is a recorded moral offense against God whose forgiveness or penalty determines one's standing before him.
God declares that whoever sinned against him will be blotted from his book; sin is a personally accountable act against God that results in the sinner's removal from the record of the living.
God announces he will visit the people's sin upon them on the appointed day; the sin is deferred in its full punishment but remains recorded, awaiting God's act of reckoning.

God's character includes forgiving sin alongside iniquity and transgression, yet the guilty are not cleared; sin is one category in a cluster of moral offenses that require divine forgiveness, which God freely grants while still upholding justice.

Iniquity here is something God forgives yet also punishes, with its moral weight extending through generations — it carries inherited consequence across family lines to the third and fourth generation.

Transgression is listed alongside iniquity and sin as one of three moral categories God forgives, yet its weight is serious enough that the guilty are not automatically cleared and its impact can pass to descendants.
Sin is openly acknowledged as a collective inner failure — 'our sin' — tied to the people's stubbornness, and Moses pleads for divine pardon so that God will still claim them as his own.

Iniquity is paired with sin as a shared moral burden borne by the whole people; Moses confesses it honestly and pleads for pardon, linking the removal of iniquity to continued covenant relationship with God.

Sinning here is an unintentional act — the will did not deliberately defy God's command, yet the deed still constitutes real sin and requires a prescribed remedy, showing sin is not limited to willful intent.

Guilt here is not self-contained — the priest's sin propagates outward, bringing guilt upon the whole people, showing that a leader's inner moral failure has communal consequences beyond the individual.

The anointed priest's sinning is not merely personal; the act implicates the entire community in guilt, so atonement requires a costly offering, indicating the gravity sin carries in a person of sacred responsibility.

Sin here is a specific committed act by the priest that requires a defined ritual remedy — a bull without blemish — establishing that sin creates an objective moral debt demanding satisfaction before God.

Guilt here arises after the congregation comes to realize what they have done; the inner recognition of guilt follows the act, suggesting conscience engages after the fact when the violation surfaces to awareness.

Sin is something that can be hidden or unknown and then 'becomes known,' implying it has an objective existence independent of awareness, and its disclosure triggers the community's obligation to make atonement.

Sin is presented as a fact that 'becomes known' to the assembly; once recognized, it demands a communal sin-offering response, showing sin as an objective moral reality requiring corporate acknowledgment and remedy.

A leader's guilt is realized after an unintentional violation; the inner moment of realization is named as the trigger for the remedial process, marking conscience as the faculty that activates accountability.

A leader's sinning, even when unintentional, is a real violation of divine command; the verse stresses that ignorance of the act does not nullify the sin, which must still be addressed through sacrifice.

Sin is something that may remain unknown until it is 'made known' to the offender; once awareness arrives, the person must bring a specified offering, underscoring that sin has an objective status demanding response.

Sin becomes actionable once it is made known to the individual; the verse treats sin as an established moral fact whose recognition by the conscience obligates the person to bring a prescribed atonement offering.

Sin is something for which the priest makes atonement on the offender's behalf; once atonement is complete, forgiveness follows, showing sin as a relational breach with God that can be fully resolved through the prescribed ritual.

An ordinary person's guilt, like that of a leader, arises through unintentional violation and is confirmed when conscience brings realization; the guilt is real and must be addressed regardless of the lack of deliberate intent.

Common people sin unintentionally by doing what is forbidden; sin is not restricted to leaders but applies to every individual, and each bears responsibility to seek atonement once the violation becomes clear to them.

Sin is made known to the offender, triggering the obligation to bring a female goat as offering; the sin is described as something the person 'committed,' giving it a concrete, personal character that demands ritual resolution.

Sin here is a committed act that is made known to the individual; the double reference — 'for his sin which he has committed' — emphasizes the personal ownership of the transgression and the specific remedy required.

Sin is something committed by the individual for which priestly atonement brings forgiveness; the ritual sequence demonstrates that sin creates a debt before God that must be cleared through a substitutionary offering.

Sin is the specific act for which priestly mediation achieves atonement and forgiveness; the verse frames sin as a relational rupture with God that the sacrificial system is designed to repair on the individual's behalf.

Sin here springs from willful silence — a witness who knows the truth but refuses to testify; the inner faculty of conscience is engaged because the person knows the matter, making the failure to act a chosen moral wrong.

Iniquity here is the moral burden the silent witness must 'bear' personally; it is the consequence that settles on the person as a result of the failure to speak, showing iniquity as a weight carried within the individual.

Guilt is realized after contact with uncleanness that was initially hidden from the person; the inner realization of guilt follows discovery, showing that conscience responds when knowledge comes, even for unwitting violations.

Guilt again emerges from a hidden violation that becomes known; the moment of coming to know triggers realization of guilt, demonstrating that conscience acts upon knowledge as the bridge between the act and moral responsibility.

Guilt arises from a rash oath spoken with the lips, even if the implications were hidden at the time; when the person comes to know it, guilt is realized, linking the spoken word to inner moral accountability.

Guilt is the internal state recognized and then verbally confessed; realization leads to confession, showing guilt as moving from inner awareness through to the will's response of honest acknowledgment before God.

Sin is something that has been committed and then confessed; the act of verbal confession implies that sin is not merely external behavior but an inner moral reality that must be consciously owned and declared.

Sin here is addressed through a compensation offering brought to the Lord; the priestly atonement that follows shows sin as creating a debt requiring both material restitution and spiritual reconciliation with God.

Sin is the moral wrong for which a compensation offering is brought; it is something 'committed' by the individual, and the sin offering is the prescribed means by which priestly mediation achieves atonement.

Sin is a committed wrong requiring atonement through sacrifice, here adjusted for the offerer's poverty; the accommodation of financial means shows that every person, regardless of status, bears responsibility for their sin.

Sin is a committed act for which priestly atonement brings divine forgiveness; the sequence — sacrifice, priestly mediation, forgiveness — shows sin as a relational deficit with God that the ritual system is designed to resolve.

Sin here is the specific act for which atonement is made and forgiveness granted; its pairing with 'committed' confirms sin's personal, volitional character even when unintentional, requiring priestly intercession for resolution.

Sin committed by a person too poor for animal sacrifice may still be atoned through a flour offering; the verse underscores that sin's moral weight is constant across social classes, though the remedy is scaled to capacity.

Sin in any of the listed cases is addressed comprehensively through priestly atonement, resulting in forgiveness; the breadth of 'any one of these things' shows sin covers a wide range of specific inner and outward failures.

Sin is the moral liability that priestly atonement resolves; once atonement is accomplished, the person is forgiven, showing sin as a definable offense against God that the sacrificial system is equipped to fully clear.

Sinning here is an unintentional misappropriation of holy things, a specific form of inner faithlessness toward God even when deliberate intent was absent, requiring a guilt offering as restitution before the Lord.

Being unfaithful here is characterized as a 'breach of faith' involving the holy things of the Lord; even when unintentional, this faithlessness is serious enough to require a guilt offering, showing it as a fundamental violation of loyalty to God.

Unfaithfulness is described as a 'breach of faith' committed against God's holy things; it represents a failure of inner loyalty and integrity toward God, demanding both a substitutionary sacrifice and financial restitution.

The sin committed against holy things requires concrete restitution — restoring the value plus a fifth — alongside priestly atonement; sin here has both a relational dimension requiring forgiveness and a material dimension requiring repair.

Guilt here is realized even without prior knowledge of the sin; the person must bear their iniquity once awareness comes, showing that guilt is an objective moral state that exists independently of subjective awareness.

Sin is committed unknowingly yet remains real; the person who 'did not know it' still bears iniquity, demonstrating that sin's moral reality is not dependent on conscious intent but on the objective violation of divine command.

Iniquity here is the burden the individual must 'bear' as a consequence of unwitting sin; it is a weight that falls on the person once guilt is realized, showing iniquity as a personal moral load carried before God.

Guilt here is declared as an objective fact — 'he has indeed incurred guilt before the Lord' — confirming guilt as a real moral standing before God that exists regardless of the person's subjective perception of the act.

Sin here is specifically tied to a breach of faith against the Lord expressed through social wrongs — deception, robbery, oppression of a neighbor — showing that sin against one's neighbor is simultaneously a moral failure before God.

Being unfaithful here involves active deception and exploitation of a neighbor; the unfaithfulness is directed against the Lord even though it is enacted through social wrongdoing, revealing that relational betrayal of others is also betrayal of God.

Unfaithfulness here is a breach of faith against the Lord enacted through deceiving a neighbor in commercial or custodial matters; it exposes dishonesty as a form of inner disloyalty toward God that damages human relationships.

Sin here arises from deliberate lying about a found item accompanied by a false oath; this is a willful act of inner deception, showing that sin includes conscious dishonesty of the will directed against both neighbor and God.

Guilt is realized after sinning through wrongful taking or deception; this realization in the conscience then obligates active restitution, showing guilt as an inner state that, once acknowledged, demands concrete corrective action toward the wronged party.

Sinning here involves a realized inner awareness of guilt that activates the will to make restitution, showing sin's connection to conscience and the obligation it creates to restore what was wrongfully taken.

Guiltiness is tied to the moment of inner realization — the day one becomes consciously aware of the wrong done by false oath — which then demands full restitution plus a fifth, linking guilt to conscience and remedial action.
Guiltiness here is the state produced by any action that makes a person liable before God; priestly atonement is the remedy that removes this state and restores forgiveness.

The sin offering ritual assigns to the officiating priest the role of consuming what was offered on behalf of sin, situating sin as something requiring sacred priestly mediation and consumption in a holy space.

Iniquity is a moral burden the offender personally bears when he desecrates the peace offering by eating it on the third day; the wrong act transfers guilt directly onto the person, nullifying any benefit of the offering.

To sin (purify) here is used in its ritual sense: Moses applies blood to the altar's horns to cleanse it of defilement, indicating that the altar itself required purification from sin before being consecrated for service.

The sin offering for the people is a prescribed ritual act through which Aaron addresses the people's collective sinful condition, showing sin as a communal reality requiring official sacrificial remedy.

The sin offering is described as most holy and given to the priests specifically so they can bear the congregation's iniquity — the uneaten offering represents a failure of the priestly mediatory duty to address the people's sin.

Iniquity here is the moral-cultic burden of the congregation that the priests are appointed to bear on their behalf through eating the sin offering, showing iniquity as a transferable load requiring priestly mediation.

Aaron questions whether eating the sin offering under circumstances of personal grief and tragedy would have been acceptable to God, showing sin as something whose ritual remedy is inextricably tied to the inner state of the officiant.

The sin offering ritual is presented as a mediatory act requiring proper inner disposition; Aaron implies that performing it while personally stricken might render it unacceptable, linking ritual efficacy to interior condition.

Sin is grouped with transgressions and uncleannesses as the collective moral pollution of Israel that defiles even the sanctuary, requiring atonement to cleanse the holy space where God dwells among the people.

Transgression is listed alongside sins and uncleannesses as a category of corporate moral failure that pollutes the sanctuary, necessitating the Day of Atonement ritual to cleanse the holy place from Israel's accumulated wrongdoing.

All the sins of Israel are verbally confessed and symbolically transferred onto the scapegoat's head through Aaron's hands, depicting sin as a real burden that can be publicly named, carried, and removed from the community.

Iniquities are verbally confessed over the goat and symbolically placed on it, representing the entire moral debt of Israel as a concrete burden transferable through ritual confession and the laying on of hands.

Transgressions are confessed along with iniquities and sins over the scapegoat, showing transgression as a distinct but overlapping category of willful breach that is part of Israel's total moral load requiring annual removal.

Iniquities are borne by the goat and carried away to a remote wilderness, depicting them as a transferable collective burden that can be removed entirely from the community through the ritual of expulsion.

Sins are what make Israel unclean before God, and the Day of Atonement provides the comprehensive cleansing of all of them, restoring the people to a state of purity and right standing before the Lord.

The annual atonement statute addresses the accumulated sins of all Israel, establishing sin as an ongoing, recurring reality in the community's life that requires a permanent, institutionalized remedy.

Iniquity is the personal liability one bears for neglecting ritual purity requirements; failure to wash after eating an animal that died naturally results in the individual carrying the guilt of that negligence.

Perversion names sexual union with animals as a fundamental violation of created order — it is self-defiling and categorically named as perversion, indicating a corruption of both the moral and natural boundaries God established.

Iniquity here is the accumulated moral wrongdoing of the land's inhabitants, so severe that God punished it by causing the land itself to expel its people, showing iniquity as having destructive consequences even on the natural realm.

Iniquity is the personal guilt incurred by profaning what is holy to God; the person who eats a peace offering at the wrong time bears this guilt individually and faces being cut off from the community.

Injustice in the courtroom is warned against as a failure of right judgment — partiality toward either poor or powerful corrupts the judicial process; the opposite of injustice here is impartial righteousness in judging one's neighbor.

Sin is located in the will and conscience: harboring hatred in the heart without honest rebuke risks incurring sin against one's neighbor, showing sin as something that can arise from inner attitudes left unaddressed.

Sin is the specific offense committed that requires priestly atonement through a guilt offering; the verse presents sin as a concrete act already committed for which forgiveness must be obtained through proper sacrificial means.

Sin is named as the specific act committed that the atonement offering addresses; once atonement is made, forgiveness is granted, showing sin as a defined moral liability that priestly mediation can resolve.

Injustice is prohibited in commercial and legal measurement — falsifying weights or quantities is named as moral wrong, showing injustice as an act of the will that violates fairness and integrity in everyday dealings.

Perversion here is the act of a man lying with his daughter-in-law — a violation of family boundary so fundamental that it carries the death penalty, with responsibility placed on both parties as equally guilty of this corruption.
Iniquity is the guilt a man bears personally for uncovering his sister's nakedness — a disgraceful act resulting in public exclusion and the man carrying the moral weight of his incestuous crime.
Iniquity is the shared guilt borne by both parties who uncover the nakedness of a close relative, showing the crime of sexual violation of kin as a guilt that the perpetrators carry on themselves.

Sin here is the guilt borne personally by those who engage in sexual relations with an uncle's wife — a violation that brings the consequences of dying childless, linking sin directly to its punitive outcome.

Sin is the moral and cultic liability priests incur by neglecting God's charge regarding holy things — negligence in sacred duty becomes a sin that can result in death, showing sin as arising from failure of responsible will.

Guiltiness is the state that outsiders cause the priests to bear by eating holy things unlawfully, showing guilt as a burden that can be wrongfully imposed on others through improper handling of what is sacred.

Iniquity is paired with guilt as the dual burden borne when holy things are profaned by being eaten by those not authorized — the act of profanation creates both moral and relational culpability before God.

The Day of Atonement is a sacred annual occasion requiring self-affliction and a food offering — it is the communal event through which Israel's accumulated sin is addressed and moral standing before God is restored.

The Day of Atonement demands complete rest from all work, underscoring that the act of making atonement before God is incompatible with ordinary human activity — the whole day is set apart for the work of reconciliation.

Sin is the personal guilt borne by anyone who curses God — the act of blasphemy against God is named as a sin that the offender must carry, indicating it is a moral liability that rests directly on the individual.

The Day of Atonement is the fixed annual day marked by a trumpet blast throughout the land, signaling that reconciliation with God is not a private act but a national proclamation with communal and temporal significance.

Sins here are the cause of escalating divine discipline — Israel's continued disobedience results in God multiplying punishment sevenfold, showing sin as the root of the breakdown of the covenant relationship and its blessings.

Sins are the basis for escalating divine punishment — continued willful hostility toward God and refusal to listen results in sevenfold intensification of affliction, linking sin directly to the hardening of the will against God.

Sins provoke God to walk contrary to Israel and strike them with multiplied severity, showing sin as the force that transforms God's relational stance from blessing to active opposition within the covenant framework.

Sins here incite God's fury and result in sevenfold discipline — the inner dimension of sin is its power to provoke divine wrath and transform the covenant relationship into one of fierce corrective judgment.

Iniquity is a rotting force — those who survive in exile waste away because of their own iniquity and that of their fathers, showing iniquity as a moral corruption passed through generations that destroys from within.

Being unfaithful names the specific quality of the sin committed against God — treachery against the Lord, willfully walking contrary to him — which must be verbally confessed before restoration becomes possible.

Unfaithfulness is the inner quality of Israel's and their fathers' treachery against God — a betrayal of covenant loyalty that must be confessed, showing it as a breach of the deepest relational trust between Israel and God.

Iniquity here is what must be verbally confessed — both the people's own and their fathers' — before restoration can begin, showing iniquity as a generational moral debt requiring honest acknowledgment as the first step toward reconciliation.

Iniquity is something the people must make amends for, and this happens only after the heart is humbled — the verse places inner humiliation of the will as the necessary precondition for dealing with the guilt of iniquity.

Iniquity is the moral debt Israel must make amends for — rooted in their soul's abhorrence of God's statutes and contempt for his rules, showing iniquity as arising from a deep inner rejection of God's authority.

Being guilty is the inner state that follows when a person realizes they have sinned by breaking faith with God — conscience becomes the faculty through which guilt is recognized, triggering the obligation to address the wrong.

Sin here is any act of breaking faith with the Lord; the inner dimension emerges when the person 'realizes his guilt,' showing that conscience registers the offense and produces awareness of moral failure.

Being unfaithful describes the inner disposition of breaking trust — the act of sinning against others is simultaneously a betrayal of loyalty to the Lord, locating unfaithfulness at the level of the will and relational commitment.

Unfaithfulness names the quality of the act of breaking faith with the Lord; it is the inner character of the transgression — a violation of covenantal loyalty that issues from the person's will and choice.

Guilt here moves outward into obligatory action — the guilty person must confess and make full restitution, showing that bearing guilt creates a moral debt on the conscience that must be discharged through tangible repair.

Sin here is something that must be verbally confessed, placing the act of acknowledgment at the center; the person owns the sin before others, indicating that the inner recognition of wrongdoing must surface into speech and restitution.

Being unfaithful describes a wife's act of going astray from her husband, framing infidelity as a breaking of relational and covenantal trust — an inner turning away that violates the bond of loyalty owed within marriage.

Unfaithfulness names the quality of going astray — the wife's departure from faithfulness is characterized as a deep relational breach, an inner failure of loyalty toward her husband that defines the moral gravity of the act.

Iniquity here is what the grain offering of jealousy brings to remembrance before God; the suspected wrongdoing is not forgotten but placed before divine awareness, showing that iniquity carries a weight that must be examined and adjudicated.

Being unfaithful to her husband results in bitter physical consequences — the curse entering her body — showing that inner betrayal of covenantal loyalty carries tangible, embodied judgment that affects the person's whole self.

Unfaithfulness here is the hidden act of defilement against her husband that the bitter water ordeal exposes; the inner breach of loyalty becomes publicly manifest through physical consequence when guilt is confirmed.

Iniquity is assigned to the woman who is guilty — she bears it personally — while the husband is cleared; this verse shows iniquity as a burden of moral culpability that is carried by the one who committed the wrong.

Sin here is something a person 'bears' as a consequence of willful neglect of a sacred obligation; the image of carrying one's sin indicates that unaddressed guilt remains on the person and leads to being cut off from the community.

Sinning is here confessed as acting foolishly — Aaron links sin directly to a failure of wisdom and right judgment, showing that the inner dimension of this sin was a lapse of discernment that he now recognizes and acknowledges.

Sin is presented as something Aaron pleads not to have punished; having acted foolishly, he confesses sin as a state incurred by wrong action, seeking mercy rather than retribution, which reveals conscience-driven appeal to a higher moral authority.

Iniquity is something the Lord forgives out of steadfast love, yet also visits on subsequent generations; this verse places iniquity within a framework of divine justice where moral failure has an enduring impact beyond the individual who commits it.

Transgression is paired with iniquity as something God can forgive through steadfast love; here it represents willful defection against divine authority, a deliberate inner act of rebellion that requires divine pardon to be resolved.

Iniquity here is the accumulated moral failure of the people from Egypt until now, which Moses asks God to pardon; the plea shows that iniquity creates a standing moral debt before God that only divine forgiveness can remove.

Iniquity here is a burden the people must bear for forty years, corresponding to their days of faithless spying; it functions as a prolonged consequence felt through time, revealing that willful failure to trust God results in sustained experience of divine displeasure.

The people confess they have sinned, yet immediately attempt to act on their own initiative anyway; this reveals sin acknowledged in the mouth but not yet transformed in the will, as their self-directed action contradicts the repentance they verbalize.

Sinning unintentionally still requires a sin offering, showing that sin carries moral weight regardless of deliberate intent; the inner person is implicated even in non-volitional failure, and the conscience must be addressed through ritual remedy.

Sinning unintentionally produces a state requiring priestly atonement; the verse shows that even inadvertent sin creates a moral condition before God that must be corrected, and that forgiveness comes through an external act of intercession rather than solely from within.

Iniquity is said to remain 'on' the person who despises God's word and breaks his commandment; the verse locates iniquity as an abiding moral stain that attaches to the one who willfully rejects divine authority, resulting in utter severance from the community.

Moses and Aaron plead that one man's sin should not bring wrath on the whole congregation; this shows sin's potential to radiate outward from one individual and affect the wider community, raising a moral question about collective versus individual responsibility.

The sins of wicked men are contagious in their effect — proximity to their tents risks being 'swept away' with them; this shows sin as a destructive force that extends beyond the sinner to potentially engulf those associated with them.

The sinners here acted at the cost of their own lives; their identity as those who sinned is memorialized in the bronze covering of the altar, showing that sin of this gravity is permanently inscribed as a warning sign for the community.

Aaron and the priests are charged to bear iniquity connected with the sanctuary and the priesthood; this assigns them responsibility as a buffer — they absorb the moral and ritual liability that would otherwise fall on those who approach God's holy space.

Sin here is the mortal danger incurred when an unauthorized person draws near the sacred tent; 'bearing sin and dying' shows that sin is not merely moral failure but a condition of incompatibility with holiness that produces lethal consequence.

The Levites bear their own iniquity as part of their permanent role in sanctuary service; this iniquity is the liability that comes with handling the holy, and they assume it on behalf of Israel as a structural feature of their calling.

Sin is avoidable if the Levites honor God's standards for the offerings; the warning 'bear no sin' by contributing the best shows that sin attaches to careless or disrespectful handling of what is holy, and right action prevents incurring it.

The people confess 'we have sinned' in speaking against the Lord and Moses; the confession acknowledges that their complaint arose from a rebellious inner attitude, and they appeal for intercession to remove the serpent judgment their sin brought upon them.

Balaam confesses sin rooted in ignorance — he did not know the angel stood against him; the verse shows that even sin arising from lack of knowledge still requires acknowledgment and offers to reverse course, linking sin to the will's readiness to repent.

The daughters distinguish their father's death as being 'for his own sin,' not Korah's rebellion; this individuates guilt, showing that sin is personally owned and that one person's moral failure is separate and distinct from collective transgression.

The sin offering of atonement is a distinct, scheduled ritual provision; it points to the ongoing moral liability of the community before God, showing that atonement must be regularly maintained and not just addressed in moments of acute wrongdoing.

If a husband annuls a vow after hearing it, he bears his wife's iniquity; this transfers moral responsibility to him because his delayed action made her culpable — iniquity shifts to the one whose will and agency created the guilty condition.

Unfaithfulness here was incited by Balaam's counsel, leading Israel to act treacherously against the Lord at Peor and bringing plague; it shows unfaithfulness as a communal inner failure of loyalty provoked by deliberate outside manipulation.

The new generation is called a 'brood of sinful men,' showing sin as a generational inner disposition — they are characterized by it constitutionally, and their continuing rebellion intensifies divine anger already provoked by their fathers.

Sinning against the Lord here is a future possibility tied to failure to keep a commitment; the warning 'your sin will find you out' shows sin as something that cannot remain hidden — it carries inherent consequences that will surface and expose the sinner.

Sin is personified as something that will 'find you out,' giving it an active, pursuing quality; it is the moral residue of breaking a vow to God that cannot be outrun or concealed, revealing that inner betrayal has an inescapable outward reckoning.

The people confess sin against the Lord but immediately proceed to act on their own initiative; this exposes a pattern where verbal acknowledgment of sin does not produce genuine inner repentance, as the will moves forward independently of God's direction.

The iniquity of idolatrous fathers is visited on children to the third and fourth generation; this shows iniquity as a spiritually transmissible moral condition rooted in hatred of God that produces consequences extending far beyond the originating individual.

Sinning against the Lord involved making a golden calf and quickly turning from the commanded way; this shows sin as a rapid inner defection of the will away from known divine instruction, rooted in a failure of trust and obedience.

Moses prostrates himself for forty days without food or water because of the sin committed; this shows sin as provoking God's anger and requiring extreme intercessory suffering, placing the weight of the people's inner moral failure on the mediator.

Sin here is all that Israel committed in doing evil before the Lord to provoke his anger; it is an aggregate moral offense that Moses fasts over, showing sin as something that strains the relationship between the people and God and demands sustained intercession.

Moses asks God not to regard the people's stubbornness, wickedness, or sin; listing all three together shows sin as one dimension of a broader inner failure — it accompanies a stubborn will and wicked character, forming a composite portrait of spiritual rebellion.

Revolt here is what the false prophet taught — rebellion against the Lord who redeemed Israel; the inner dimension is a deliberate attempt to redirect Israel's loyalty away from God, making revolt a subversive act of the will aimed at breaking the covenant bond.

Sin originates in the heart — an 'unworthy thought' that leads to a grudging eye and refusal to give; this verse traces the sin of stinginess toward the poor from its inner source in the heart's calculation, showing conscience implicated before any outward act.

To pervert justice through bribery blinds the wise and subverts the righteous; the inner dimension is a corruption of perception and judgment — the perversion works on the cognitive faculties, distorting the ability to discern right from wrong.

Sin here refers to any offense a person has committed; the verse addresses the legal standard for establishing such charges, showing that sin as wrongdoing requires verified testimony — inner guilt must be confirmed by external evidence before legal consequence follows.

Sin is the offense in connection with which a witness must testify; the verse does not elaborate on inner content but establishes that sin requires corroborated human testimony to be legally actionable, situating it within a framework of communal accountability.

Sin here is a chargeable offense against another person requiring legal verification; a single witness cannot establish its reality, demanding two or three witnesses before guilt is formally confirmed.
Iniquity here denotes a criminal wrong committed against another person that must be legally proven; it is a concrete offense requiring corroborating testimony before a charge can stand.
Revolt here describes the wrongdoing a malicious witness falsely attributes to another; the inner act is one of treacherous accusation, weaponizing legal process against an innocent person.
Sinning here is the anticipated outcome of Israel being taught the abominable religious practices of Canaanite peoples; the will is corrupted by exposure to foreign worship, turning it against God.
Sin here is a capital offense committed by an individual—an act so grave that it warrants death and public display as a deterrent, marking the person as one who crossed the ultimate moral boundary.
Sin here is a capital moral offense that the coerced young woman has not committed; her innocence is affirmed by the involuntary nature of the act, contrasting with the aggressor's culpable will.
Sin here arises from a failure of the will to follow through on a vow made to God; delay in fulfilling a freely made commitment constitutes a moral fault that God holds the person accountable for.

Sin here is the guilt incurred by making a vow and not keeping it; by contrast, refraining from vowing altogether avoids the condition of sin, showing that sin attaches to broken intentional commitment.
Sinning here is an act that defiles the land as a whole; the prohibited remarriage is declared an abomination, and proceeding with it would bring corporate moral pollution upon Israel's inheritance.
Sin here is incurred by failing to pay a poor worker's wages on time; when the worker cries to God in need, the employer's neglect becomes a moral fault that God registers against them.
Sin here is strictly personal—each person dies for their own transgression, not another's; moral accountability is located within the individual, not transferred across family lines.

Injustice here is dishonest commercial behavior—acting with fraudulent measures; those who act this way are called an abomination to God, showing that unjust dealing in the marketplace corrupts one's standing before him.

Evil/trouble here appears in a negative declaration—the speaker has not eaten the tithe while in a state of mourning or impurity, distancing the act from any morally corrupt context and affirming clean obedience.

Injustice is here used negatively to define God's character: he is entirely without it, being just and upright by nature; the term's absence in God marks the standard against which all human injustice is measured.

Perversity here is a generational character trait—Israel is described as a perverse generation lacking faithfulness; the inner disposition is one of inverted loyalty, prompting God to hide his face from them.

Unfaithfulness here is a breach of trust directed personally against God at Meribah-kadesh; the failure to treat God as holy in public constitutes a betrayal of the covenantal relationship, with severe personal consequence.

Being unfaithful here means breaking Israel's sacred obligation by taking devoted things; Achan's individual act of will against the ban is credited to the whole people, kindling divine anger against them all.

Unfaithfulness here is Israel's corporate breach of covenant regarding devoted things; though one man acted, the faithlessness is attributed collectively, showing that a single person's inner betrayal can implicate the whole community.

Sinning here is a compound inner failure—Israel transgressed the covenant through theft, deception, and concealment; the sin is not a single act but a chain of willful moral violations against God's explicit command.

Sinning here is Achan's personal confession of deliberate wrongdoing against the Lord God of Israel; the first-person acknowledgment reveals an inner awareness of guilt and the violation of sacred obligation.

Being unfaithful here is building an unauthorized altar and turning from following the Lord; the eastern tribes are accused of a breach of faith directed against God, expressed outwardly in religious defection.

Unfaithfulness here is the concrete act of building a rival altar and turning away from God; it is characterized as a breach against God himself, showing that the heart's loyalty to God was questioned.

Iniquity here is the unresolved corporate guilt from the Peor apostasy that still clings to Israel; the community has not fully cleansed itself, and the plague that followed showed that unpurged sin has enduring communal consequences.

Breaking faith here refers to Achan's violation of the devoted things; though one individual's act, it brought wrath on all Israel, showing that treachery against God's sacred command carries communal weight.

Unfaithfulness here is Achan's single act of taking devoted things; the verse highlights that he did not perish alone for it, demonstrating that one person's inner betrayal can extend its consequences to others.

Iniquity here is the guilt-laden crime for which Achan was individually accountable, yet which had communal fallout; the verse stresses personal moral ownership while acknowledging the crime's ripple effect on all Israel.

Unfaithfulness here is an act of rebellion against the Lord that the eastern tribes call on God himself to judge; invoking God as witness shows the inner seriousness of the charge—a breach that only God can truly assess.

Unfaithfulness here is the specific breach the eastern tribes are cleared of; their not committing it is the evidence that God remains in Israel's midst, showing how closely communal well-being tracks covenant loyalty.

Sins here are transgressions that a holy and jealous God will not forgive; Joshua warns that Israel's inner capacity for persistent sin is incompatible with serving a God whose very character demands exclusive loyalty.

Transgressions here are acts of rebellion against God that he will not pardon; paired with sins, they represent deliberate covenant-breaking that Joshua warns the people's willfulness makes inevitable.

Sinning here is Israel's self-confessed forsaking of God to serve the Baals; the people acknowledge that their willful religious defection was the sin against God that brought them into the hands of their enemies.

Sinning here is a bare, unqualified confession of guilt before God; Israel submits completely to God's judgment while pleading for deliverance, showing that the inner recognition of sin opens the door to seeking mercy.

Sinning against the Lord is presented as categorically more severe than sinning against a fellow human; when sin is directed at God himself, no human mediator can intercede, revealing the irreversible spiritual danger of such rebellion.
Iniquity here is Eli's failure to restrain his blasphemous sons; his knowing inaction made the crime his own, and God declares that this moral failure will bring perpetual judgment on his household.

Sinning here is Israel's corporate confession to the Lord at Mizpah, accompanied by fasting and ritual pouring of water; the public acknowledgment marks the turning of the will back toward God after a period of apostasy.

Sinning here is again identified with forsaking the Lord and serving the Baals and Ashtaroth; the people's confession names the specific inner defection—transferred loyalty from God to idols—as the root of their oppression.

Iniquity here is an unidentified guilt—possibly Saul's, Jonathan's, or the people's—that has blocked God's answer to Saul; the use of Urim and Thummim to locate it shows that moral fault is seen as a concrete, identifiable thing residing in a specific person.

Saul's confession 'I have sinned' follows David's mercy in sparing his life again; here sinning is identified with foolish, harmful action against an innocent person, and Saul acknowledges it was a great personal mistake.

God declares that when David's son commits iniquity, discipline rather than rejection will follow—the iniquity is a moral deviation expected within the human condition, yet it triggers corrective fatherly action, not abandonment.

David's confession 'I have sinned against the Lord' is immediately met with forgiveness—sin here is directed vertically against God, and its gravity is such that its removal by God alone averts death.

Shimei pleads that the king not remember how he 'did wrong'—the perverting action is his past sinful behavior against David, which he now hopes the king's mercy will overlook rather than punish.

David's heart 'struck him' after numbering the people—conscience generated inward conviction of sin before any external judgment came, showing sin as something the heart recognizes and condemns from within.

David asks God to 'take away the iniquity of your servant,' treating iniquity as a moral burden or crime that remains upon him until God actively removes it—its weight prompted the inner strike of conscience.

David confesses 'I have sinned and I have done wickedly,' taking full personal ownership of the sin while pleading that innocent people not bear the consequences—sin is located in his own will and agency, not theirs.

Solomon asks God to 'forgive the sin of your people Israel,' treating sin as the corporate offense that caused exile and whose forgiveness is the necessary condition for God to restore them to the land.

The people are called to 'turn from their sin' as the condition for divine forgiveness and rain; sin here is something one can actively turn away from, implying it is a chosen direction of the will that can be reversed.

Solomon grounds the prayer in the universal truth that 'there is no one who does not sin,' establishing sin as an inevitable feature of human inner life whose consequence—captivity—requires a provision for repentance and forgiveness.

Solomon enumerates 'all their transgressions committed against God,' treating transgression as the accumulation of specific willful violations against God that require comprehensive forgiveness before restoration can occur.

Judah's sinning is described as provoking God to jealousy—their acts of transgression function as a relational offense directed against God, surpassing even the evil of prior generations.
Judah's sins provoked God to jealousy more intensely than their fathers had, indicating that sin here is measured by its escalating relational offense against God.
The king's sins are tied directly to his heart not being wholly true to God, locating sin in the inner allegiance of the heart rather than only in outward behavior.
The king's sin is described as a path walked and then transmitted to Israel, showing sin as both a personal direction of the will and a socially propagated influence.
The king's sin is something he personally inhabited and then caused Israel to inhabit, presenting sin as a morally infectious pattern of conduct rooted in his own choices.
Jeroboam's sinning is the root cause of divine anger, showing that personal transgression that draws others in has an escalating effect on God's relational response.
Jeroboam's sins are cited as the cause of God's anger against Israel, portraying sin as a burden that triggers divine wrath with ongoing consequences for the nation.
The king walked in Jeroboam's sin and spread it to Israel, showing sin as a habitual path of the will that is consciously followed and passed on.
Sin here is a defined pattern associated with Jeroboam that the king chose to walk in and caused Israel to walk in, depicting it as a transmissible moral legacy.

The people's sins provoke God to anger; here sin is framed as the collective moral content that generates God's relational displeasure.

Obadiah protests his innocence by asking what sin he could have committed to deserve being handed to Ahab for death, framing sin as an act that would justly draw lethal punishment.

Ahaziah followed the sin-path of his father, mother, and Jeroboam, showing that sinning can be a will aligned to inherited patterns, compounding transgression across generations.
Moab's rebellion against Israel after Ahab's death is the political-military expression of transgression—a breaking away from a covenant or vassal relationship that constitutes an act of revolt.
Jehoram clung to Jeroboam's sin and did not depart from it, depicting sin as something to which the will clings with persistence, refusing the alternative of turning away.

Moab's rebellion upon Ahab's death is a concrete political defection, a breaking of submission that constitutes transgression against the ruling authority.

Edom's revolt from Judah's rule is a concrete act of breaking political submission, presenting transgression as a defection in which a subordinate people asserts independence against an authority.

Jehu did not turn aside from Jeroboam's sins despite his reforms, indicating that sinful practice—specifically golden calf worship—can persist in the will even when other evils are addressed.
Jehu's failure to depart from Jeroboam's sins—specifically the golden calves at Bethel and Dan—shows that sin can remain as a fixed object of continued practice even in a reforming leader.

Jehoahaz followed Jeroboam's sins and did not depart from them, depicting sin as a religious-political path that the will adheres to without turning, passed from leader to leader.
Jeroboam's sins are the moral pattern Jehoahaz followed and did not leave, presenting sin as an entrenched mode of conduct that demands an act of will to abandon—and he never made that choice.
Israel did not depart from Jeroboam's sins but walked in them, showing sin as a communal pattern of behavior persisted in by collective will, even alongside partial reform.
The sins of Jeroboam's house remained as a continuing moral pattern that Israel walked in, with the Asherah's survival in Samaria showing that sin leaves concrete, persistent objects in its wake.
Jehoash did not depart from Jeroboam's sins but walked in them, again depicting sin as a deliberate path of conduct the will chooses to remain on rather than forsake.
All the sins of Jeroboam are named as what the king walked in without departing, showing sin as a comprehensive inherited pattern that defines the king's whole moral direction.

Jeroboam II did not depart from all of Jeroboam son of Nebat's sins, showing that sin can persist as an unbroken moral inheritance that a leader's will never moves to reject.

The sins of Jeroboam the son of Nebat remain the named moral standard Jeroboam II failed to depart from, presenting sin as a defined inherited pattern that calls for an act of turning that never came.
Zechariah followed his fathers in doing evil and did not depart from Jeroboam's sins, showing sin as a generationally transmitted moral orientation that the will accepts by not turning from it.
Jeroboam's sins are the continuing moral pattern Zechariah clung to like his predecessors, depicting sin as a fixed legacy that requires active departure—and he chose not to depart.
Menahem did not depart from Jeroboam's sins all his days, showing sin as a lifelong orientation of the will—an entire life's direction shaped by refusing to turn from inherited transgression.
All the sins of Jeroboam persisted throughout Menahem's reign without departure, showing sin as a total moral pattern that defined the entirety of his days as king.
Pekahiah did not turn away from Jeroboam's sins, presenting sin as a fixed moral direction that the will refuses to abandon—turning away being the expected and withheld response.

Sin here is an entrenched pattern of will—Menahem's failure to turn away from Jeroboam's sins shows sin as a persistent moral direction passed down and actively maintained, not merely an isolated act.

To sin here describes a wilful clinging to an inherited corrupt course; Pekahiah did not depart from Jeroboam's sins, showing sin as a chosen continuation of a wrong path that also drew others into it.

To sin here is something Jeroboam caused Israel to commit—he drove them from following the Lord, making their sin a socially imposed corruption of will and devotion, not merely personal.

Sin here is a comprehensive lifestyle pattern—Israel walked in all Jeroboam's sins and did not depart from them, showing sin as an active, persistent moral direction embraced by the whole community.

Hezekiah's confession 'I have done wrong' is a personal acknowledgment of moral failure directed toward a superior; it shows sin as the inner recognition of a fault that demands reparation and submission.
To sin here is the corrupting influence of Manasseh's idolatry on all Judah; his inner devotion to abominations became a force that caused an entire nation's will to be bent away from God.

To sin here is tied directly to Manasseh causing Judah to do evil; sin is presented as something one person's corrupt leadership actively transmits to others, shaping the moral conduct of a whole people.

Sin here is the specific moral wrong Manasseh led Judah into, distinct from but compounding his shedding of innocent blood; it is an additional dimension of corporate corruption that results in evil conduct before God.

To sin here refers to Manasseh's whole recorded wrongdoing—sin is presented as a committed act belonging to him personally and memorialized in official records, giving it a fixed, accountable character.

Sin here is the totality of Manasseh's offenses treated as a documentable, historical fact recorded for communal memory; it names the definitive moral character of his reign as something that endures in record.

Being unfaithful here is Achan's breach of the sacred ban; it is an act of inner disloyalty toward a divine command that brought ruin on the whole community, identifying him permanently as 'the troubler of Israel.'

Breaking faith here involves the heart's transfer of allegiance—the tribes prostituted themselves to the local gods, showing unfaithfulness as a deep inner defection from the God of their fathers to false rivals.

Unfaithfulness here is named as the direct cause of Judah's Babylonian exile; it is the settled inner disposition of covenant disloyalty that produced the ultimate communal consequence of being torn from the land.

Iniquity here is what David asks God to remove; paired with his confession of foolish action, it refers to the moral weight and liability attached to his wrong choice, something that must be actively taken away by God.

David's sin here is self-consciously owned—'It is I who have sinned'—and distinguished from the people's innocence; the verse shows sin as a personal moral act of the will for which the sinner bears unique accountability.

Sin here is the communal offense that caused exile from the land; Solomon prays for divine forgiveness and restoration, showing sin as the relational breach with God that must be removed before physical return is possible.

Sin here has natural consequences—drought comes because the people sinned against God; turning from that sin, paired with prayer and acknowledgment of God's name, is the necessary inner movement to restore relationship and blessing.

Sin here is the offense for which servants need forgiveness; its resolution comes through God hearing, forgiving, and teaching the good way—showing sin as a moral disorientation that corrupts the path one walks.

To pervert here is confessed alongside sinning and wickedness as the inner moral distortion the exiles acknowledge; it names the twisting of one's conduct away from what is right, seen as a distinct element requiring repentance.

Sin here is the relational breach God promises to forgive in response to humbling, prayer, seeking, and turning; it is the root moral condition that has damaged the land and the covenant, requiring both inner turning and divine action to heal.

Transgression here is Israel's ongoing rebellion against the Davidic house, described as a political and covenantal defection that has persisted to the narrator's present day, rooted in a decisive act of broken loyalty.

Being unfaithful here is the inner spiritual condition of Rehoboam's Israel that directly triggered Shishak's invasion; it is the heart's abandonment of covenant loyalty to the Lord that exposed the nation to foreign judgment.

Injustice here is explicitly absent from God's character—there is no injustice in the Lord—establishing it as the perversion of judgment through partiality and bribery that judges must fear God to avoid in their own conduct.

To incur guilt here is a communal legal liability before God; judges must warn the parties so that guilt is not brought upon Israel through unresolved disputes—showing guilt as a contagious moral weight attached to failure to instruct rightly.

To transgress here is Edom's political revolt against Judah's authority; in context it expresses a decisive breaking away from an established rule, reflecting the root sense of willful separation from a governing relationship.

Revolt here by Edom and Libnah is causally linked to Jehoram forsaking God; the verse shows transgression as a defection that mirrors its instigator's own inner abandonment of the Lord, spreading outward as political fragmentation.

Guiltiness here is the direct cause of divine wrath—Judah's abandonment of God's house and service of idols produced a guilt that drew God's anger upon them, showing guilt as the accumulated moral liability of corporate apostasy.

Sin here is explicitly personal and non-transferable—each person dies for their own sin, not another's; the verse limits guilt to the individual moral agent, showing sin as a specifically owned inner-moral accountability.

Being unfaithful here flows directly from pride—when Uzziah grew strong his heart became proud and he trespassed into priestly domain; unfaithfulness is rooted in an inner spiritual pride that oversteps God-given boundaries.

Being unfaithful here is identified by the priests as Uzziah's act of usurping priestly duties; it is a transgression of sacred order that brings no honor from God, showing unfaithfulness as a willful crossing of divinely appointed limits.

Guiltiness here is a confrontational rebuke—the prophet challenges Israel's intent to enslave Judah by pointing to their own existing guilt before God, showing guilt as a personal moral debt that disqualifies one from compounding another's.

Guilt here is already great and accumulating—bringing captives in would add more guilt against the Lord on top of existing sins; guilt is portrayed as a growing moral weight with a threshold that intensifies divine wrath.

Sins here are the existing offenses stacked alongside the guilt being warned about; they represent the ongoing moral record of Israel's conduct before God that makes any further wrong act especially dangerous given the fierce wrath already present.

Being unfaithful here is Ahaz's personal spiritual defection that caused national humiliation; his unfaithfulness to the Lord is named as the inner cause of God humbling all Judah, showing how one leader's heart-state brings collective consequence.

Unfaithfulness here is intensified by 'very'—Ahaz was exceedingly faithless to the Lord—and is paired with making Judah sin; it is the defining inner posture of his reign, the deep disloyalty that drove both his own and his nation's downfall.

Being unfaithful here intensified under pressure—Ahaz became yet more faithless in the time of his distress; the verse reveals unfaithfulness as a hardened inner disposition that deepens rather than breaks when circumstances call for repentance.

Being unfaithful here is described as the fathers forsaking God and literally turning their backs on his dwelling; unfaithfulness is an embodied inner rejection—a deliberate turning away of face and will from the presence of God.

Unfaithfulness here is Ahaz's past reign-defining disposition; the temple utensils discarded during his faithlessness are now restored, showing unfaithfulness as a period of inner apostasy with lasting material consequences that must be remediated.

The sin offering here is a ritual object—goats brought before the assembly—over which hands are laid; sin is conceived as a transferable moral burden that can be ceremonially placed onto a substitute presented before king and community.

The sin offering here is a priestly act using sacrificial blood on the altar to make atonement for all Israel; sin is the moral liability that requires a blood-mediated ritual remedy ordered by the king on behalf of the whole nation.

Being unfaithful to God is portrayed as a willful act of the will that produced a devastating consequence — God made the unfaithful ones a desolation, visible to all, as a warning against repeating the same inner betrayal.

Manasseh's sin is recorded alongside his idolatrous acts and his faithlessness, showing sin as a comprehensive inner disposition expressed outwardly in building forbidden shrines, reversed only when he humbled himself.

Manasseh's faithlessness is listed beside his sin as a distinct inner quality — a breach of loyalty to God expressed through idolatrous construction, contrasted with the humility that ultimately moved God to respond.

Amon's guiltiness is portrayed as an accumulating inner state — he refused to humble himself as his father had, and instead piled up guilt progressively, showing the will actively moving away from repentance.

Unfaithfulness here is a corporate inner disposition of the priesthood and people, expressed by following the abominations of the nations and defiling God's holy house — an extreme, exceeding degree of betrayal.

Unfaithfulness is the inner quality driving the officers and priests to follow national abominations and pollute the sanctuary, showing it as a disposition that corrupts both the person and what they are entrusted to protect.

Unfaithfulness here is the inner breach expressed through intermarriage with foreign peoples, which contaminated the holy community; the leaders were most culpable, making it a failure of those in whom faithfulness was most required.

The faithlessness of the returned exiles caused those who feared God's word to tremble and sit appalled, showing that unfaithfulness produces grief and dread in those whose conscience is attuned to God's commands.

Guilt is pictured as an overwhelming inner weight — it has risen above the head and mounted to the heavens, producing shame and an inability to lift one's face before God, locating guilt in the conscience and affecting posture before him.

The community's iniquities are so vast they have risen above the head, producing shame and inability to face God — iniquity here is an accumulated inner-corporate burden that overwhelms the conscience.

Guiltiness is portrayed as a persistent generational state — 'great guilt' stretching from the fathers to the present day — that has produced concrete consequences: captivity, sword, plundering, and utter shame.

Iniquity here is the cause behind national catastrophe — kings, priests, and people have been handed over to defeat and shame because of it, making iniquity the inner root of communal destruction across generations.

Great guilt is acknowledged as the deserving cause of all the suffering that has come, yet God's judgment was less than it deserved — guiltiness here is evaluated against divine justice, revealing a conscience aware of moral debt.

Iniquity is presented as a standard against which God's punishment was measured and found to be restrained — the community's iniquities deserved more than they received, showing iniquity as a quantifiable moral deficit before God.

Guiltiness is the inner state that makes standing before God impossible — the people stand before him in their guilt with no recourse, showing guilt as a barrier between the human conscience and God's holy presence.

Being unfaithful to God is confessed as a specific act of will — marrying foreign women — yet hope is affirmed, showing that unfaithfulness, though serious, does not foreclose the possibility of renewed loyalty.

The faithlessness of the exiles produced in Ezra profound grief — he fasted and mourned through the night — showing that awareness of others' unfaithfulness burdens the conscience and spirit of those who take God's covenant seriously.
Intermarriage with foreign women is directly named as the act that increased Israel's collective guilt — showing guiltiness as a communal inner burden that grows through specific willful decisions.
Breaking faith with God is identified as the concrete act of marrying foreign women, linking unfaithfulness directly to a volitional choice that increases corporate guilt before God.
The community confesses they have 'greatly transgressed in this matter,' showing transgression as a collective, acknowledged moral failure that requires structured corporate process to address.

Guilt here is directly tied to a remedial act — offering a ram — and to a pledge to put away foreign wives, showing guiltiness as a recognized moral state that demands both acknowledgment and costly action.
Guiltiness is the condition requiring a guilt offering — a ram of the flock — showing it as an inner moral state that must be ritually addressed before reconciliation with God is possible.

Sinning against God is confessed collectively and personally — both the nation and Nehemiah's own household — showing sin as a shared inner failing that Nehemiah owns and brings before God in sustained intercession.
The sins of Israel are confessed openly before God in persistent prayer, showing sin as an inner moral reality that requires honest acknowledgment directed toward God as the one sinned against.
Acting corruptly against God is expressed through failure to keep God's commandments, showing corruption as an inner disposition of disobedience that damages the relationship with God.

Unfaithfulness is presented as a conditional state Moses warned against — if the people are unfaithful, scattering among the nations follows — making it the inner disposition whose outward result is exile and dispersion.

The enemies' sin is called upon not to be covered or blotted out, because they have provoked God to anger — sin here is presented as a moral record before God that his anger confirms, demanding divine memory rather than forgiveness.

Guilt is something that can be covered or exposed before God's sight — here Nehemiah asks God not to conceal it, showing guilt as a moral record that provoking God's anger makes visible and liable to judgment.

Sinning is presented as a trap — enemies hired Shemaiah to frighten Nehemiah into a fearful act that would constitute sin, showing sin as a yielding of the will under pressure that ruins one's reputation before others.
Israel's sins are confessed alongside the iniquities of their fathers in a solemn assembly, showing sin as a corporate and intergenerational inner failing that required open public acknowledgment.

The iniquities of the fathers are confessed alongside the people's own sins, showing iniquity as a moral inheritance from ancestors that the present generation acknowledges and takes responsibility for.
Sinning against God's rules is set alongside stubborn shoulder and stiff neck — a hardened will — showing sin here as a willful inner refusal to submit to God's word despite repeated warnings.

Sins are named as the cause of foreign kings ruling over the people's bodies and livestock, showing sin as the inner failure whose outward consequence is the loss of freedom, dignity, and productive life.

Solomon's sin is attributed to being led astray by foreign women, showing sin as an inner vulnerability of the will that even a beloved and uniquely gifted king could not resist when exposed to corrupting influence.
Acting treacherously against God is equated here with the great evil of marrying foreign women, showing unfaithfulness as a volitional act of betrayal that violates covenant loyalty to God.

Queen Vashti's wrong is framed as a perversion of right conduct that reaches beyond the king to all officials and peoples, showing the act as a breach of social order with inner willful defiance at its root.

Job fears his children may have sinned inwardly — cursed God in their hearts — showing sin as a possible orientation of the heart that may not be externally visible but is morally real and requires intercession.
Despite devastating loss, Job did not sin — showing that sin would have consisted in falsely accusing God; his inner restraint of tongue and heart kept him from that moral failure.
Job did not sin with his lips — locating the potential sin specifically in speech — showing that even under extreme suffering, inner composure and submission to God can prevent sinful expression.

Trouble does not arise from the ground spontaneously — implying it has a moral or human source — placing the origin of affliction in the inner realm of human action rather than in impersonal natural forces.

Injustice is silenced when the poor have hope — showing injustice as a force that oppresses the vulnerable and can only be quieted when God acts to restore the powerless, giving hope its contour against injustice's cruelty.

The verse speaks of peace in one's tent and finding nothing missing in one's fold — the term cha.ta here carries the sense of missing or lacking, applied to material security; the inner-being content of sin as missing the mark is minimal in this verse's specific application to livestock.

Brothers are called treacherous by likening them to wadis that vanish when needed most — showing treachery as a failure of inner loyalty that betrays those who counted on it, revealing an inconsistent will incapable of reliable faithfulness.

Job pleads that no injustice be done to him, staking his vindication on it — showing injustice as an act that could be done against him in judgment, making it a violation of the integrity that his conscience insists he possesses.

Job denies that injustice is on his tongue, appealing to his palate's discernment — locating potential injustice in speech and linking its avoidance to an inner faculty of moral perception that can distinguish right from wrong.

Job asks what harm his sin could possibly do to God, the watcher of mankind — showing sin here as a moral act directed against God that raises a question about its effects on the divine-human relationship and Job's burden of being divinely observed.

Job asks God to pardon his iniquity before death makes it irreversible — showing iniquity as a moral debt between Job and God that only divine forgiveness can remove, with urgency driven by human mortality.

Job appeals for pardon of his transgression alongside iniquity, framing both as things God could forgive — showing transgression as a willful breach that stands between Job and God, whose pardon Job urgently desires before death.

Bildad suggests Job's children sinned and were delivered into the hand of their transgression — showing sin here as a volitional act whose consequence becomes a self-executing judgment, the transgression itself becoming the agent of punishment.

Transgression is personified as a hand into which God delivers sinners — showing it as an active moral force whose grip becomes the punishment for those who sinned, making transgression both the act and its own consequence.
Job perceives God as actively searching him for sin, implying sin is something that can be discovered within a person when scrutinized by a divine investigator.
Job portrays iniquity as something God is urgently seeking out within him, framing it as a hidden criminal fault that divine inquiry pursues.
Job describes sinning as an act that triggers God's constant watchfulness and forecloses any possibility of acquittal, placing the inner act under relentless divine surveillance.
Iniquity here is the guilt-bearing record that God refuses to clear; it persists as an unresolved charge that divine justice will not release.
Zophar declares that Job's actual guilt exceeds what God has exacted, framing iniquity as a moral debt whose full weight God has graciously not imposed.
Zophar urges Job to actively expel injustice from his hand and refuse to let it take up residence in his household, treating it as a willful inner disposition that must be deliberately removed.
