Job accuses his friends of speaking falsehood and deceit on God's behalf, implying that injustice here is the inner dishonesty expressed through twisted theological speech.
Job pleads for God to enumerate and specify his sins, showing that sin is something he cannot himself fully account for and needs divine disclosure to understand.
Job asks God to count his iniquities, presenting them as specific criminal acts against him whose number and nature remain unclear to him without divine revelation.
Job asks God to make his transgression known to him, showing that transgression is a specific willful breach he cannot fully perceive without God's illumination.
Job laments that God makes him inherit the iniquities of his youth, portraying early-life crimes as a burden carried forward that God now uses to write bitter judgments against him.
Job envisions a future where God would no longer surveil his every sin, implying that sin is currently the object of constant divine monitoring that makes life unbearable.
Job pictures iniquity as something God would mercifully cover over, contrasting it with its current state of being sealed and stored against him as an accusatory record.
Job imagines his transgression sealed in a bag—a legal image of a preserved indictment—that in a hoped-for restored relationship God would set aside rather than prosecute.
Eliphaz charges that Job's iniquity actively schools his mouth, locating iniquity as an interior moral corruption that drives and shapes his crafty, self-justifying speech.
Eliphaz portrays injustice as so thoroughly internalized by the corrupt person that he drinks it like water—an instinctive, appetite-driven consumption of wrongdoing.
Job says God has handed him over to the ungodly, using the term to describe morally lawless people into whose power God has surrendered him, highlighting their capacity for harm.
Job says his spirit is broken, using the language of destruction to describe an inner collapse of vitality—the person's deepest self has been ruined, with death already looming.
Bildad identifies the devastated dwelling as the home of the unrighteous—one who does not know God—defining the unjust person by their spiritual ignorance of the divine.
Job warns that divine wrath brings punishment via the sword, with iniquity here functioning as the cause deserving that punitive consequence—guilt carries its own judicial sentence.
Zophar declares that heaven and earth will expose the wicked man's iniquity, presenting it as a hidden criminal act that cosmic forces will ultimately force into the open.
Eliphaz asserts that Job's iniquities are endless in quantity, using their abundance to argue that such accumulation of moral crime accounts for the severity of Job's suffering.
Eliphaz urges Job to remove injustice far from his tents as the condition for restoration, treating it as a concrete moral wrong that must be actively expelled from one's living space.
The verse compares Sheol's claim on sinners to drought consuming snow water—those who have sinned are swiftly and completely swallowed by death, as if by a natural force.
Wickedness here is portrayed as something broken like a tree—a structural force that collapses and is forgotten, showing injustice ultimately carries the seeds of its own destruction.
Job vows his lips will not speak falsehood or deceit, implicitly defining injustice as the dishonest speech he refuses to utter—locating it in the organ of verbal moral action.
Job equates his enemy with the unrighteous, using the term as an ethical category of the person who rises against the innocent—the unjust one defined by hostile wrongdoing.
Job recalls breaking the power of the unrighteous who preyed on others, portraying the unjust person as a predatory figure whose capacity for harm requires forcible restraint.
Job asserts that calamity and disaster are appointed for the unrighteous, framing the unjust person as one whose moral state inevitably draws divine retributive consequence.
Job categorizes lust for another man's wife as an iniquity deserving judicial punishment, placing this moral crime squarely within the sphere of legally punishable wrongdoing.
Job identifies secret idolatry as an iniquity punishable by judges because it constitutes faithlessness toward God above—the crime's seriousness lies in its vertical betrayal.
Job insists he has not allowed his mouth to sin by cursing an enemy, locating the restraint of sin in the disciplined control of speech even against those who wronged him.
Job denies hiding his iniquity in his heart, distinguishing honest self-disclosure from the inner concealment of moral failure that others practice to avoid accountability.
Job denies concealing his transgressions as others do, indicating that transgression is something the inner person is tempted to hide rather than openly acknowledge.
Job's claimed words 'there is no iniquity in me' assert a state of inner moral cleanness—iniquity here is what one might or might not carry inside as a constitutive moral quality.
Job's claim to be 'without transgression' presents transgression as a specific willful breach that a person either has or lacks, here denied as absent from his character.
The restored man publicly confesses 'I sinned,' showing that sin is acknowledged as a past personal act from which one has turned and for which one did not receive the just consequence.
The confessor admits he 'perverted what was right,' locating the twisting of moral rectitude as the inward act of distorting truth and justice that accompanied his sinning.
Job protests that despite being without transgression—without willful breach of right—he is counted a liar and suffers incurable wounds, highlighting the injustice of undeserved accusation.
Elihu affirms it is unthinkable that God would commit injustice or wrong, establishing God's inner moral character as structurally opposed to the doing of any injustice.
The hypothetical penitent vows 'I will not offend any more,' using the language of destruction/harm to describe the decision to cease acts that damage one's relationship with God.

The penitent asks to be taught unseen wrongs and pledges to stop doing injustice once it is identified, showing injustice as a moral failing one must be enlightened to recognize and then willfully abandon.
Elihu accuses Job of adding rebellion to his existing sin, portraying sin as a base upon which further defiant acts accumulate, compounding the moral offense against God.
Elihu says Job adds rebellion as transgression on top of his sin, identifying transgression as a willful, defiant act of revolt that escalates and multiplies the original offense.

Job's implied question—what profit is there in not sinning?—treats sin as an alternative course of action whose avoidance he is questioning the benefit of, revealing spiritual confusion about moral incentive.
Elihu argues that sinning harms only the sinner, not God himself, detaching sin from any power to injure the divine and directing attention back to its effect on the human agent.
Even multiplied transgressions accomplish nothing against God, showing that transgression—however accumulated—is bounded in its impact and cannot reach or damage the divine being.
God declares to sufferers their transgressions, identifying arrogant behavior as the specific content of those transgressions—rebellion against divine order expressed through proud self-assertion.
Elihu asks who can charge God with having done wrong, implying that injustice is categorically impossible for God—his sovereignty and way are self-authorizing, beyond moral accusation.
The blessed man avoids standing in the way of sinners, treating sinners as a social-moral class whose path and company exert a gravitational pull that shapes inner orientation and conduct.
Sinners are excluded from the congregation of the righteous at judgment, showing their identity as those whose inner moral character bars them from standing before God and the upright community.
Sinning is a danger that can arise from anger; the remedy is inward pondering on one's bed in silence, placing the will and reflective mind as the faculty that guards against it.
Guilt is something the enemies must be made to bear as a consequence of their abundant transgressions and rebellion against God, linking it to judicial accountability and moral weight.
The abundance of transgressions is the direct ground for being cast out by God, showing that the accumulation of wilful rebellion produces an outcome of divine expulsion.
Injustice is located in the hands as the instrument of wrongdoing; the psalmist protests innocence by declaring there is no such wrong in his hands, grounding moral accountability in deeds.
Iniquity is something the psalmist actively kept himself from, presenting it as a moral force to be guarded against through the will, and blamelessness is the result of that self-restraint.
Great transgression is portrayed as something that can exercise dominion over the person; the psalmist prays to be kept innocent of it, framing it as a ruling, enslaving inner force.
Those who act treacherously without cause will be put to shame, contrasting them with those who wait on God; the treachery is a deliberate, wanton inner disposition that leads to disgrace.
The sins of youth are remembered by God and the psalmist pleads they not be held against him, showing sin as a record rooted in past personal history that weighs on the present relationship with God.

Transgressions from youth are things the psalmist begs God not to remember, presenting them as rebellion lodged in personal history that threatens the relationship with God unless covered by steadfast love.

Sinners are not abandoned but are the objects of God's instruction in the way, showing the sinner's inner condition as one of lostness or moral disorientation that can be redirected by divine teaching.

The psalmist's guilt is described as great and requiring pardon for the sake of God's name, showing iniquity as a weighty moral debt that exceeds the person's ability to remedy without divine forgiveness.
Sins are placed alongside affliction and trouble as burdens the psalmist carries, and he asks for forgiveness for all of them, linking sin to inner and outward distress that only God can relieve.

Sinners are those whose company is deadly to the soul; the psalmist fears being swept away with them, framing the sinner's character as a destructive communal identity that endangers even the innocent bystander.

Iniquity causes physical and vital failure: strength fails and bones waste away because of it, showing it operates as a draining force within the person that corrupts both vitality and bodily wholeness.
Sin is something that can be covered, and its covering brings blessedness; here sin is a moral stain on the person whose concealment by God restores the person to a state of happiness and acceptance.
Transgression is forgiven and its forgiveness is the basis of blessedness, showing it as a moral liability held against the person that, once cancelled, transforms the person's standing before God.

Iniquity is something God may or may not count against a person, and its absence in the spirit—alongside no deceit—defines the blessed inner state, locating iniquity squarely in the spirit's moral account.

Sin is acknowledged openly to God after the psalmist stopped covering it; this confession of sin as a personal moral reality held before God results in divine forgiveness, showing sin as something requiring transparent acknowledgment.

Iniquity is something the psalmist had tried to conceal but then confessed; God forgives the iniquity of sin when it is disclosed, showing it as a morally concealed reality that must be uncovered for restoration.

Transgression is confessed verbally to God with the will and voice engaged; the act of confession is what brings forgiveness, showing willful acknowledgment as the turning point in the relation between transgression and pardon.
Being guilty (condemned) is the judicial outcome for those who hate the righteous, linking guiltiness to one's hostile disposition toward the upright rather than to a single act.
None who take refuge in God will be condemned/guilty, contrasting the fate of those condemned in v.21; taking refuge in God is the inner disposition that keeps one from being counted guilty.

Transgression is personified as speaking to the wicked deep in the heart, with no fear of God before the eyes, locating transgression's origin in the innermost heart as an internal voice that drives out reverence for God.

Iniquity is self-concealed through self-flattery in the eyes; the wicked person's inner perception is so distorted by pride that he cannot see or hate his own iniquity, showing iniquity as self-perpetuating through deluded self-assessment.

Injustice is the defining trait of wrongdoers who seem to prosper; the psalmist warns against envying them, showing that the inner response of envy toward injustice is itself a spiritual danger to be resisted.
Transgressors face complete destruction and their future is cut off, showing that persistent transgression as a settled way of life leads to total ruin with no remaining hope or posterity.

Sin causes the absence of health in the bones, linking it directly to bodily breakdown; sin here operates as a force that corrupts physical wholeness from within, manifested as disease or bodily decay.
Iniquities are likened to a heavy burden gone over the head, an image of being overwhelmed; they press on the person with crushing weight, engaging the will and body under an unbearable moral load.

The verb 'bowed down' (twisted/bent) describes the psalmist's physical and inner posture of being utterly crushed and going about in mourning, showing that sin's consequence is a distorted, downcast inner and bodily state.

Sin produces sorrow: the psalmist is sorry for his sin, engaging the emotion of grief as the inward response to moral failure, showing sin as something that stirs genuine regret and contrition in the conscience.
Iniquity is confessed openly before God with the word 'I confess my iniquity,' showing the will and voice engaged in owning the moral failure as a prerequisite to seeking healing and restoration.

Sinning with the tongue is a specific danger the psalmist guards against by resolving to keep watch over his mouth, showing sin as a temptation that can erupt through speech and must be restrained by deliberate resolve.

Transgressions are a source of shame and disgrace; the psalmist prays for deliverance from all of them lest they make him the scorn of fools, showing transgression as a moral burden that exposes him to contempt.

Sin is the ground for God's disciplining rebukes that consume what is precious to a person, showing iniquity as the direct cause that triggers divine correction and the wasting away of one's cherished possessions.

Sin offering is mentioned as a ritual God has not required, suggesting the term here refers to the sacrificial institution rather than to an inner-being characteristic; the verse contrasts external rites with an open, obedient ear.

Iniquities have overtaken the psalmist and caused his heart to fail; they overwhelm his inner capacity for sight and resolve, showing iniquity as a force that clouds perception and collapses the will and heart.

Sinning against God is acknowledged as the moral reality underlying the psalmist's need for healing; sin is directed personally toward God, giving it a relational character that makes divine grace the only remedy.

The unjust man is deceitful, and God is asked to deliver the psalmist from him; injustice here is paired with deceit as a combined inner disposition of the oppressor that corrupts both relationship and truth.

The iniquity of those who cheat surrounds the psalmist as a source of fear in times of trouble, showing iniquity as an external hostile moral force that creates inner dread when it encircles the vulnerable person.

Transgressions are to be blotted out by God's abundant mercy; the psalmist's plea after grievous sin shows transgression as a moral record requiring divine erasure that only steadfast love and mercy can accomplish.

Sin is a defilement from which the psalmist must be cleansed; it is conceived as a moral impurity clinging to the person that requires thorough washing, engaging God's purifying action on the inner and outer person.

Iniquity is a deep inner stain requiring thorough washing; the psalmist pleads to be washed from it, showing iniquity as a pervasive moral contamination that penetrates the person and can only be removed by God.

Sin is ever before the psalmist's inner awareness; he cannot escape its presence in his conscience, showing it as a persistent self-accusation that occupies thought and memory continually after moral failure.
Transgressions are known and acknowledged by the psalmist; they are recognized by the mind and conscience, showing transgression as something the inner person is fully aware of and cannot deny.
Sinning is directed against God alone; this vertical orientation reveals that sin at its core is a relational offense against God's person and authority, not merely a horizontal social wrong.

Sin is present at the very moment of conception, showing it as a condition inherited at the origin of personal existence, not merely an act chosen but a state the person is born into from the beginning.

Iniquity characterizes the womb-context of the psalmist's birth; he was brought forth in iniquity, locating it at the deepest constitutional origin of human existence, prior to any personal act of will.

The psalmist asks to be purged and washed so as to be whiter than snow; sin is implied as the moral impurity that requires ritual-like cleansing, and the desired result is complete purity in the inner and outer person.

Sins are viewed by God's face that sees them; the psalmist asks God to hide his face from them, showing sin as a visible moral reality before God's gaze that requires divine non-recognition for the person to find peace.
The psalmist pleads for God to avert his gaze from accumulated sins and erase all iniquities, presenting them as a recorded moral debt requiring divine cancellation.
Sinners are portrayed as those who have strayed from God but are capable of returning; the psalmist's own restored experience becomes the basis for teaching them back to God.
Transgressors are those who have broken from God's ways yet remain reachable; the psalmist envisions teaching them, implying their transgression is a state of willful departure that can be reversed.
Injustice here flows from the inner denial of God; the fool's heart-assertion that God does not exist produces corrupt, abominable conduct, showing that inward godlessness drives outward wrongdoing.
Injustice is devised inwardly in the heart before it is enacted by the hands, showing it originates as a deliberate inner scheme that then flows outward into violent action on earth.
The psalmist declares personal innocence, insisting no sin of his provoked the attack; sin here is a moral cause that would justify the enemies' aggression, but none exists in him.
Transgression is invoked as a potential justification for being hunted; the psalmist's denial of any transgression on his part frames it as a moral ground for deserving hostility, which he lacks.
Iniquity is cited as a personal moral fault that could legitimately provoke pursuit; the psalmist stresses no such fault exists in him, making the enemies' aggression groundless.
Treacherous plotting is an inward disposition translated into deliberate scheming toward evil; God is called to punish those whose will is bent on such deceitful, malicious conspiracy.
Sin expressed through the mouth — in curses and lies — is shown to originate from inward pride, with speech as its outward vehicle and the whole person becoming ensnared by their own words.
Injustice is actively plotted through deliberate inward searching; the verse ties it explicitly to the deep inward mind and heart, showing it as a calculated, intentional inner project.
Iniquities are described as forces that prevail against the person, exerting inner pressure that overwhelms; only God's atoning action can overcome what guilt does to the inner person.
Transgressions are presented as accumulated wrongs requiring divine atonement; God's act of atoning for them is the only resolution to what the community has incurred against him.
Guiltiness is fully transparent to God even when hidden from others; the psalmist acknowledges that the wrongs done — his moral failures — are entirely known to God, removing any concealment.
Punishment is heaped upon punishment as a consequence of iniquity; the term here carries the sense of deserved penalty, with the plea that enemies receive no relief or acquittal from God.
Acting treacherously is framed as a potential inner betrayal through speech; had the psalmist voiced his doubts, it would have constituted a faithless act against the community of God's people.
Sinning is shown as a persistent, willful act of the will directed against God even amid his provision; it is compounded by open rebellion against the Most High in the wilderness.
Sinning persisted despite witnessed wonders, revealing it as rooted in unbelief of the heart; the people's refusal to believe God's works kept them in ongoing sin against him.
Iniquity is the moral offense that would have provoked full divine wrath, but God's compassion led him to atone for it instead; the term marks the crime that endangered the people's existence.

Acting treacherously describes a deliberate turning away and faithless reversal of loyalty, likened to a twisted bow that cannot be trusted; it is a volitional, ancestral pattern of inner unfaithfulness to God.
Former iniquities are moral crimes inherited or accumulated from the past that God is asked not to hold against the present generation; they carry weight before God that could bring judgment.

Sin is what the community needs deliverance from; it is presented as something requiring divine atonement, and God's willingness to atone for it is tied to his own name's honor, not just the people's need.
Injustice here is a pattern of corrupt judicial action — judging unjustly and favoring the wicked — showing it as an ongoing inner disposition corrupting the exercise of authority.
Sin is something that can be covered — concealed by divine mercy — after already being incurred; God's covering of it is paired with forgiveness of iniquity, indicating complete moral restoration.
Iniquity is the moral crime of God's people that required his act of forgiveness; God is credited with lifting this guilt, indicating it was a real relational debt before him.
Injustice here is embodied in the 'wicked' as an enemy force; the verse does not detail its inner nature but presents it as a power that the promised king will not be overcome by.
Iniquity is presented as deserving physical punishment; God promises to discipline it with stripes, showing it as a moral crime with unavoidable corrective consequences even for the covenant line.
Transgression is punishable by the rod; it is treated as a concrete act of defiance within the covenant relationship that God will not ignore, disciplining even his anointed's descendants for it.
Iniquities and secret sins are set fully in God's presence and light, meaning nothing of inner moral failure is hidden from him; the divine gaze exposes what conscience may not even fully know.
Trouble here describes the wearisome, painful character of human life — its span marked by toil and suffering — reflecting the inner-experiential burden of existence under mortality's weight.
Injustice is used negatively to declare God's absolute moral purity; there is no unrighteousness in him, making his character the structural opposite of the injustice found in mortal beings.

Iniquity is a personal moral condition requiring forgiveness; it is paired with disease, suggesting it affects the whole person — moral and physical — and God's forgiveness addresses the inner moral debt.
Sin is something that could warrant proportional divine punishment, but God mercifully withholds what is deserved; the verse reveals sin as incurring a debt that grace exceeds.
Iniquities are offenses that could justly earn retribution; God's refusal to repay according to them reveals his mercy as exceeding the moral claims that iniquity generates against the person.
Transgressions are removed by God to the furthest imaginable distance from the person; the spatial image conveys total separation of the person from their moral offenses through divine act.

Sinners are those whose character is defined by sin, making them incompatible with the renewed earth; the psalmist's desire for their removal reflects the structural tension between sinful persons and God's good creation.

Sinning is openly confessed as the shared moral record of the community and its ancestors; it is the first in a triple acknowledgment of cumulative moral failure, implicating the will across generations.

Committing iniquity is placed alongside sinning and doing wickedness as one of three layers of moral failure; the term suggests a twisting or distorting of what is right, operating within the community's conscience.
Iniquity is tied to rebellious purposes of the will — inner stubbornness — that led the people to be brought low; it is presented as the inner moral cause of their repeated humiliation and suffering.
Iniquities are shown as the direct moral cause of physical affliction; the fools' suffering results from their own moral choices, connecting inner wrongdoing to outward, bodily consequence.
Sinful ways are the path of foolishness that leads to affliction; transgression here describes the wayward moral conduct whose consequence is suffering, linking willful straying to unavoidable harm.

Wickedness (injustice) is silenced when God's righteous acts are displayed; it is personified as a voice that shuts its mouth before divine justice, showing it as fundamentally unable to stand before God's order.

Prayer itself can become sin when offered by the guilty; the verse depicts a moral inversion where religious expression, rather than gaining mercy, counts as further offense due to the person's inner guilt.
Sin is remembered before God as a transmitted moral stain from mother to child; the psalmist prays it not be blotted out, using it as a basis for continuing divine judgment against the enemy.
Iniquity of the fathers is held in divine memory as a charge against their descendant; it functions as an inherited moral record before God that the psalmist invokes to warrant ongoing judgment.

Doing no wrong is the behavioral mark of those who walk in God's ways; injustice is defined by its absence — it is what the obedient heart and life do not do, making it the structural opposite of covenant faithfulness.

Sinning against God is resisted by storing his word in the heart; the verse locates the battle against sin in the inner person — the heart as the site of stored truth that guards the will from moral failure.
The faithless are those whose inner disposition has led them to abandon God's commands; their treachery evokes disgust in the psalmist, marking it as a morally repugnant state of broken loyalty.
Injustice is a contagious pressure; prolonged exposure to its rule over the land could tempt even the righteous into doing wrong, showing it as an external force capable of corrupting the inner will.
Iniquities, if fully marked by God, leave no one able to stand before him; their weight is so comprehensive that divine accounting of them would make every person indefensible before his holiness.
Iniquity is here the accumulated moral debt of Israel that only God's redemptive act can fully remove, framing it as a bondage requiring divine liberation rather than human effort.
Sinners are depicted as morally aggressive agents who actively solicit others into wrongdoing; the inner danger is the will yielding consent to their enticement.
Perversity here manifests as twisted, corrupted speech—words bent away from truth—that wisdom's deliverance must actively rescue the person from following.
Perversity here is an inner delight and rejoicing in evil's distortion; the will and emotions are positively oriented toward what is morally inverted.
Acting treacherously is shown as a defining character trait that brings ultimate uprooting and loss of place; the treacherous are contrasted with the upright as those cut off from blessing.
Perversity here is expressed through devious, crooked verbal output; the command to put it far away implies it is a tendency of speech that the will must actively reject.
Sin is portrayed as a self-entangling force—the cords of his own sin bind the wicked—showing that wrongdoing becomes an inward trap that captures the sinner himself.
Iniquity is the snare that catches the wicked person; his own criminal acts become the very cords that hold him fast, illustrating self-destructive moral bondage.
Perversity originates in the heart and drives the mind to devise evil continually; it is a deep inner disposition that produces outward discord and destructive schemes.
Perverted speech is listed alongside pride and arrogance as something hated by wisdom itself, marking it as a moral wrong rooted in a character that has departed from what is straight.
Sinning against wisdom is self-harm; the one who misses wisdom injures his own soul, showing that the inner consequence of this failing is self-inflicted damage.
Transgression here is the offense or wrong between persons that love has the power to cover over, in contrast to hatred which stirs such wrongs into open strife.
Sin is the destination or outcome that the wicked person's gain leads toward, contrasted with life as the destination of the righteous—framing sin as a destructive end-state.
Transgression multiplies when words are many; unrestrained speech is the pathway through which inner wrongdoing overflows, while self-control of the lips keeps it in check.
Perversity inhabits the tongue of the wicked and will be silenced by judgment; it is the inner moral corruption that distorts what the mouth produces, opposite of wisdom.
Perversity is what the wicked know and produce with their mouths, contrasted with the righteous knowing what is acceptable; it reflects a moral disposition that shapes verbal output.
Treachery is a crookedness of character that destroys its possessor; where integrity guides the upright, the treacherous are undone by their own moral distortion.
The treacherous are taken captive by their own lust; their inner craving becomes the agent of their downfall, showing treachery as bound to destructive inner desire.
The sinner here faces certain recompense on earth—if even the righteous are repaid, the sinner cannot escape; the verse implies that sinning carries inescapable consequence.
A twisted mind results in social contempt; the inner faculty of thought or understanding is morally warped, and this distortion of the mind is what earns the person despise.
Transgression of the lips ensnares the evil man—his own sinful words trap him—showing that verbal wrongdoing acts as a self-sprung moral snare with real consequence.
The treacherous person's deepest inner craving is for violence; their desire is set toward harm, revealing an appetite oriented against others' wellbeing.
Sin is an overturning force that brings down the wicked; it acts as an inner structural flaw that topples rather than guards, contrasted with righteousness that protects.
Sin is described as perverting or overthrowing the wicked, indicating that wrongdoing operates as a corrupting, destabilizing force that ruins those who follow it.
Despising the word brings self-destruction on the person; the inner act of contempt for divine instruction is directly linked to ruin brought upon oneself.
Treachery here defines a way of life whose trajectory ends in ruin; acting treacherously is a chosen path whose inner direction leads inevitably to destruction.
Sinners are pursued by disaster as their inescapable lot; sin is their defining identity and the source of calamity that tracks them, contrasted with the righteous rewarded with good.
The sinner's accumulated wealth cannot be passed on to his own; his moral condition disqualifies him from legacy, and what he gathers is redirected to the righteous.
Sinning is here identified with despising one's neighbor; the inner attitude of contempt toward the vulnerable constitutes the sin, while generosity to the poor stands as its opposite.
Sin is a national reproach—a collective moral shame—showing that wrongdoing has social and communal impact beyond the individual, degrading people as a whole.

Iniquity is a moral liability that requires atonement through steadfast love and faithfulness; the fear of the Lord enables turning away from it, framing it as something requiring both relational and reverential remedy.
Perversity characterizes the dishonest man whose inner character produces strife and relational destruction; his corrupt disposition breaks apart even close friendships.
Perversity is planned internally and executed through deliberate physical signals; the winking eye and pursed lips show a calculating inner will bent toward evil outcomes.
Transgression can be covered—hidden and forgiven—as an act of love; repeating it drives people apart, showing the offense has relational weight that determines communal bonds.
Loving transgression is here equated with loving strife; the inner affection for wrongdoing is shown to produce conflict and to lead toward self-destruction.
A brother offended—transgressed against—becomes fortified in resistance; the relational wound created by transgression hardens the inner will and fractures the deepest bonds.
Sinning here takes the form of haste without knowledge—acting without understanding leads to missing one's way; the inner failure is impulsive will outrunning discernment.
A man's folly perverts or ruins his own path, yet his heart then rages against God; the perverting act originates in the self but the heart wrongly redirects blame outward.
Transgression here is the offense that a person of good sense overlooks; the capacity to absorb rather than retaliate for wrongdoing is presented as glory and inner strength.
Sinning against the king—provoking his anger—forfeits one's life; the verse shows that sinful provocation of authority carries mortal personal consequence.
Sin is lodged in the heart—no one can claim their heart is pure or they are clean from sin—pointing to sin's deep interior location within every person's inner being.
Haughty eyes and a proud heart are themselves identified as sin; the inner attitudes of pride and arrogance are not merely precursors to sin but constitute it directly.
The Righteous One perverts or throws down the wicked to ruin; God acts as the one who overturns those whose perversity defines them, bringing moral order through judgment.
The traitor serves as a substitute ransom for the upright, implying that treachery carries a cost that is ultimately borne by the one who acts faithlessly rather than by the innocent.
Trouble and calamity are what a person reaps from sowing injustice; the verse frames evil-doing as a seed planted by an active inner choice that yields destructive harvest.
Injustice is the seed sown that produces calamity; the verse frames moral wrongdoing as an active, deliberate planting whose consequences are inescapable and proportional.
The traitor's words are overturned by God's watchful eyes; treachery produces speech that God actively frustrates, showing that faithless inner character cannot hide from divine scrutiny.
God overthrows the words of the traitor, acting against perverted speech; the perverting is located in the treacherous person's words, which God's knowledge exposes and defeats.
Sinners are those whose apparent prosperity tempts the heart to envy; the warning is against letting the inner faculty of desire be drawn toward those defined by wrongdoing.
The treacherous woman multiplies traitors among men; treachery here is a morally contagious condition spread through predatory deception that draws others into faithless behavior.
Drunkenness corrupts the inner person: the heart, seat of thought and speech, pours out twisted, distorted words — perversity originates in the heart and expresses itself through the mouth.
Sin is located in the deliberate mental act of devising folly — the inner scheming of the foolish mind is itself sinful, not merely its outward result.
The treacherous person is one whose inner character is fundamentally unreliable — like a broken tooth or slipping foot, they fail precisely when depended upon in crisis.

A land's transgression produces societal instability and multiplied rulers — the corporate inner rebellion against order directly generates external chaos, contrasted with a wise man's stabilizing effect.
Transgressions are concealed by the will, an inward hiding that prevents prosperity; confessing and forsaking them — acts of will and honest inner disclosure — opens the path to mercy.
Transgression is shown to be an act of the will bent by appetite — even the trivial incentive of a piece of bread is enough to move a person to do moral wrong.

The transgressor suppresses conscience by self-justifying speech, verbally denying that theft from parents is transgression — the inner refusal to name the act exposes a willfully hardened moral perception.
Transgression acts as a trap that ensnares the evil man — it holds him captive, contrasted with the freedom and joy of the righteous, showing transgression's enslaving grip on its possessor.

Transgression increases proportionally with the wicked population — it is a communal moral contagion that multiplies as wickedness grows, eventually meeting its downfall when the righteous witness it.
Anger and wrath in the inner person are generative of transgression — one given over to anger becomes a source that causes much sin in others, showing wrath as a root of multiplied transgression.
The unjust man embodies a moral character so antithetical to righteousness that he is an abomination to the righteous — injustice and integrity are shown as fundamentally opposite inner orientations.
Guilt is incurred by a specific social wrong — slandering a servant — and brings a curse upon the slanderer; the inner state of being guilty is triggered by willful harmful speech against another.

The sinner is defined by not pleasing God, and as a result receives only toil that ultimately benefits someone else — sinning is contrasted with God-pleasing as an inner orientation determining one's life outcome.
The mouth is the instrument through which the inner person leads itself into sin — rash speech before the divine messenger brings guilt that provokes God's anger and destroys the speaker's work.
Sinning is presented as a universal condition of all human beings — even the righteous man who does good cannot escape sinning, showing sin as an inescapable dimension of human inner nature.

The sinner is one who does not please God and so lacks the inner disposition to escape the seductive trap — his failure is contrasted with the God-pleaser who does escape, locating sin in a relational inner stance.
The sinner repeatedly does evil yet may live long — but despite outward prosperity, the contrast with those who fear God reveals that sinning is fundamentally the absence of the fear of God within.

The sinner faces the same external fate as the righteous — death levels all distinctions — showing that sinning does not alter one's mortal destiny, though it defines one's moral character in contrast to the good.
One sinner has the destructive capacity to undo the benefit of great wisdom — the sinner's inner character is shown as a powerful negative force that can overwhelm much accumulated good.

Great offenses or sins can be laid to rest by calmness — the inner quality of composure when facing a ruler's anger counteracts the escalation that sin produces, showing sin as something that can be de-escalated by wise inner self-control.

Transgressing against God is portrayed as children rebelling against a nurturing father — despite being raised and cared for, the will turned against the one who gave it life, making transgression an act of relational betrayal.
Being sinful is a national identity — the people are laden with iniquity, have forsaken God and despised his holiness, showing sin as a deep collective inner condition of estrangement from God.

The people are burdened or laden with iniquity as a heavy load — the criminal dimension of their inner condition is presented as a weight they carry, paired with their forsaking of God and corruption of conduct.

Persistent revolt despite punishment is shown to have made the whole head sick and the whole heart faint — continued inner rebellion produces comprehensive interior moral and spiritual collapse affecting mind and will.
Sins are portrayed as deep, vivid stains — scarlet and crimson — requiring divine action to become white as snow; the inner moral pollution of sin is not self-removable but needs God's cleansing.
Sinners are grouped with rebels and those who forsake the Lord — their inner character as persistent evildoers leads to their being broken and consumed in divine judgment.

Rebels — those who transgress — are paired with sinners and those who forsake the Lord, all facing destruction together; transgression is an inner orientation of abandonment of God that ends in judgment.

Their sin is openly proclaimed on their faces and declared without shame like Sodom — the inner moral corruption has risen to the surface, fully public, with conscience no longer restraining its display.

Sin is something people actively drag toward themselves with deliberate effort like hauling with cart ropes — it is a willful inner commitment to wrongdoing, pulled closer through persistent desire rather than stumbled into.
Iniquity is drawn along with cords of falsehood — the criminal conduct is bound together with deception as its means, showing that inner dishonesty is the vehicle by which iniquity is pursued.

Isaiah's sin is located at his lips — the organ of speech and prophetic function — and is atoned for by a divine act of touching; the sin required external atonement, not self-correction, to be removed.

Guilt is borne on the lips of the prophet and is taken away by the seraph's touch — the inner guilt-state is removed by divine action, shown as something that had rendered Isaiah unfit until cleansed.

Wickedness is depicted as a consuming fire that burns uncontrollably through everything in its path — it is an inner force that, once released, spreads destruction far beyond its origin with self-perpetuating energy.

Sinners are those who fill the land and must be destroyed from it on the day of divine wrath — their inner character as evildoers makes them objects of fierce judgment, identified as inseparable from the land's desolation.

The world's iniquity — its criminal wrongdoing — is directly linked to arrogant pride; God will punish both, showing iniquity as an inner orientation of self-exaltation and ruthlessness that provokes divine retribution.

This verse describes unceasing blows and unrelenting persecution rather than revolt in the sense of the M10 cluster — the term here applies to the aggressor's relentless striking rather than evidencing inner-being content for revolt/sa.rah clearly.

The guilt of fathers is transmitted as a moral inheritance that justifies slaughter of their sons — guilt has a generational weight that can fall upon descendants, showing its capacity to extend its consequences through family lines.

The traitor is defined by betrayal as the core act — in a vision of coming violence, treachery is named as an active inner disposition that produces harm, paired with the destroyer as its natural counterpart.

The term here describes being physically bowed down by anguish — the prophet's body is bent/twisted by the overwhelming inner pain of the vision, showing the twisting as an effect of extreme anguish on the person's constitution.

This iniquity is so severe it will not be atoned for until death — the criminal dimension of the people's conduct is declared by God as unforgivable within their lifetime, showing iniquity as a terminal inner-moral condition.

The earth's inhabitants suffer and are scorched because of their guilt — guilt is a condition they bear that brings a devouring curse upon the whole earth, producing catastrophic outward consequences from the inner moral state.

Treacherous ones are identified by their repeated, compounded act of betrayal — the threefold emphasis on 'the traitors have betrayed with betrayal' reveals treachery as a settled, intensified inner character that keeps producing its act.

The earth's transgression is a heavy burden that lies upon it, causing it to stagger and fall — transgression is portrayed as a weighty moral load whose pressure brings collapse and prevents recovery.

The inhabitants' iniquity is what God comes to punish — their criminal conduct includes bloodshed that the earth has concealed, showing iniquity as a hidden inner-moral contamination that cannot remain covered before divine justice.

Sin is something to be fully removed — its removal is the 'full fruit' of atonement, requiring the destruction of idolatrous altars; sin is an inner-moral stain whose complete cleansing demands the elimination of its outward instruments.

Jacob's guilt is atoned for through purification from idolatry — the guilt-state requires concrete acts of religious repudiation to be removed, showing guilt as a condition that clings until its specific causes are dismantled.

Perversity here is a cognitive-relational inversion — the creature's mind is so twisted it denies the creator made it and denies his understanding, reversing the proper order of creature-dependence and creator-authority.

To sin here is to falsely make a man out to be an offender through words — the sin is a deliberate perversion of truth and justice in public proceedings, using speech as the instrument of wrongful condemnation.

Sin is something that can be deliberately accumulated — stubborn children add sin to sin by executing plans and alliances apart from God's will, showing sin as a willful inner multiplication of rebellion against divine guidance.

Iniquity is likened to a structural flaw — a bulging crack in a high wall — that builds silently until sudden catastrophic collapse; the criminal conduct has a hidden inner pressure that inevitably breaks outward in destruction.
Revolt here is a deep, willful turning away from God — the people have gone far in their departure, and the call to 'turn back' presupposes the will and agency needed to reverse that inner direction.
Sin is located in the hands as the active agents of idolatry — the people crafted silver and gold idols, making sin a product of deliberate human making rather than passive failure.

The scoundrel's destructive scheming is a planned, intentional inner disposition — his wicked devices target the vulnerable poor through calculated deceit, showing destruction as a willed moral strategy.

Treachery here is shown as a defining identity trait of the destroyer — they have acted treacherously toward others, yet the coming reversal reveals treachery as a moral posture that ultimately turns back on its practitioner.
Sinners in Zion are seized with fear before God's consuming fire — their inner response of dread reveals that their sinful identity makes them unable to stand in God's holy presence.
Iniquity here is something carried by the people like a burden of guilt — its forgiveness is paired with physical healing, suggesting it weighs on the whole person until divinely removed.
Sin is cast behind God's back — it is treated as something fully removed from divine memory and reckoning, contrasting with the bitterness it had caused, now replaced by delivered life.
Sins here are a debt or penalty that Jerusalem has paid double for — they have a measurable weight of consequence that required full satisfaction before God's comfort could begin.
Iniquity is something that can be pardoned and fully discharged — its forgiveness marks the end of Jerusalem's warfare with God and the opening of a new relational season.
Sinning against God is defined here by the refusal to walk in his ways and obey his law — it is volitional disobedience of the will that directly caused Israel's national devastation.
Sins are portrayed as a burden that God himself bears — they weary and exhaust divine patience, framing sin as something that strains the relationship from the human side through accumulated offense.
Iniquities are wearisome to God — they are the accumulated moral failures of the people that burden him, implying iniquity has a relational weight that fatigues even divine forbearance.
Sins are blotted out and no longer remembered by God — their forgiveness is entirely God's sovereign act done for his own sake, not dependent on human merit or memory.
Transgressions are blotted out as records God chooses not to retain — the image of erasure shows transgression as a recorded moral debt against God that only he can fully cancel.
Sinning is traced back to the first father — establishing sin as an ancestral, generational failure in the will that has persisted through appointed mediators who also transgressed.
Transgression is committed by mediators — those who should have represented the people faithfully before God instead rebelled against him, showing transgression as a betrayal of spiritual office.
Sins are compared to mist that is dispersed — they are fully dissolved by divine act, and their removal is the ground for the call to return, showing sin as the obstacle to restored relationship.
Transgressions are like a cloud blotted out — their erasure is God's redemptive act, and their disappearance restores the relational path back to him that transgression had obscured.
Transgressors are addressed as those who need to fix what they know firmly in mind — their rebellion is connected to a failure of memory and resolve, addressed by a command to recall and stand firm.
Treachery is shown as a disposition God foreknew before Israel's birth — it is a constitutional bent of the will, not an occasional failure, making it an inborn character trait of unfaithfulness.
Being called a rebel from before birth shows transgression as an identity-level characteristic — it is not merely behavioral but named as a defining personal label known to God from the womb.

Iniquities are the direct cause of Israel being sold into exile — they function as the transactional price of separation, showing iniquity as the inner offense that produced concrete relational and national rupture.

Transgressions caused the mother to be sent away — they are the active force behind relational expulsion, framing transgression as the specific offense that breaks covenant bonds and causes divine separation.
Iniquities crushed the servant on behalf of the people — they are moral crimes that carry real punitive weight, transferred from the guilty onto the innocent servant who bears their full consequence.

Transgressions pierced the servant — they are the specific moral offenses of the people that required the servant's suffering, showing transgression as having a violent, physical cost when borne vicariously.
The iniquity of all is collectively laid upon the servant — it arises from every person's self-directed turning away, and the servant bears its full accumulated weight on behalf of all.
The servant is struck down for the transgression of the people — his death is the price paid for their moral rebellion, and almost no one perceives or considers this substitutionary reality.

The servant bears iniquities as a load that the righteous one carries so others can be accounted righteous — iniquity here is the moral debt of the many that the servant absorbs through his knowledge and suffering.
The servant bore the sin of many as a weight requiring intercession — he identified with transgressors to the point of death, taking on the sin that separated them from God.

The servant is numbered among transgressors and makes intercession for them — transgression here marks those on whose behalf the servant suffers and pleads, showing it as the condition requiring his mediation.
Transgression is a hereditary identity — the mockers are called 'children of transgression,' meaning rebellion is their inherited nature, passed down as a family character rather than isolated acts.

Iniquity of unjust gain provoked God's anger and hiding of his face, yet the person continued backsliding in the way of his own heart — showing iniquity as a self-chosen inner direction that persists even under divine discipline.
Sins are things the people are unaware of or suppressing — they must be declared aloud to them, suggesting sins here are self-concealed moral failures that require prophetic exposure.
Transgression is something the people need to hear declared — it is not self-perceived, requiring a loud prophetic proclamation to bring it to their awareness and conscience.
Sins hide God's face and cause him not to hear — they produce relational distance at the deepest level, severing both divine attention and divine response, making prayer ineffective.
Iniquities create a separation between the people and their God — they function as a structural barrier in the relationship, isolating the person from God's presence and communication.
Injustice/wickedness is expressed through the tongue that mutters it — it flows outward through speech as a persistent inner moral disorder made audible in words.
Iniquity is on the fingers — it is enacted through the body's active members, showing it as a moral corruption that expresses itself through deliberate physical action alongside blood-stained hands.
Sins testify against the people as witnesses — they are known inwardly ('we know our iniquities') and stand as evidence before God, showing the conscience's awareness of accumulated moral failure.
Iniquities are something the people consciously know — they are present with the people and acknowledged by the conscience, showing iniquity as internally recognized guilt that cannot be denied.

Transgressions are multiplied and present with the people as constant companions — their proliferation before God and abiding awareness in the conscience show transgression as an openly admitted, ongoing condition.
Revolt is spoken from the heart alongside lying words — it originates in the inner person as a deliberate inner orientation of denial and defiance against God, expressed outwardly in speech.
Transgressing is the first item in a chain of inner-to-outer moral failures — it leads to denying the Lord and turning back from God, showing transgression as the initiating act of rebellion.

Transgression is something one can turn from — the Redeemer comes specifically to those in Jacob who make that inner volitional turn, showing transgression as a condition requiring deliberate repudiation.

Injustice/wrong is something God hates as the structural opposite of the justice he loves — here it appears as robbery and wrong paired together, standing against God's commitment to faithful recompense and covenant.

Sinning happened in response to God's anger, and the people have persisted in it a long time — sin here is both a response to divine displeasure and a prolonged condition of the will that raises doubt about salvation.

Iniquities carry the people away like wind carrying a leaf — they are an external and internal force that sweeps persons helplessly from God, showing iniquity as a power that overcomes even those who see their own uncleanness.
Iniquities are a hand that causes the people to melt — they are an active power that has melted resolve and agency, while God's hidden face compounds the inner paralysis they produce.
Iniquity is something God is entreated not to remember forever — its ongoing divine memory sustains wrath, so the plea is for God's memory of it to cease, freeing the people from its consequences.

Iniquities are inherited and compounded across generations — the people's present crimes together with their fathers' are measured together, showing iniquity as a multigenerational accumulation requiring full repayment.

To sin is here the defining identity of the person who dies cursed at a hundred years old; sinning is contrasted with long, blessed life, marking the sinner as one whose inner rebellion forfeits the flourishing promised in the new creation.

Transgression against God results in permanent, unquenchable judgment on the dead; the rebels' inner defiance is evidenced in their enduring, visible condemnation, serving as an abhorrence to all who observe them.

Guilt is incurred by those who consumed what was consecrated to God; the inner state of guiltiness immediately draws divine disaster, showing that violating what is holy produces objective moral liability before God.

God charges that his people found no injustice or wrong in him, yet still chose to depart; the inner movement away from God is therefore groundless, driven not by any fault in God but by the will's pull toward worthless idols.
Transgression here is the willful turning of the shepherds (leaders) away from God toward Baal; it involves a failure of knowledge and loyalty by those whose role was to guide the people toward God.

Degeneracy is portrayed as an inner transformation of what was once pure and choice: a vine God planted as wholesome has turned wild from within, signifying a corrupted moral nature that produces fruit contrary to its origin.

The guilt is so deeply stained into the person that no external cleansing—lye or soap—can remove it; it remains permanently visible before God, indicating guilt as an indelible inner moral condition, not merely an outward act.

Transgression is universal here—all the people have rebelled against God—and yet they presume to contend with God, revealing a will that is both guilty and self-justifying in its inner posture toward him.

Sinning is compounded by the inner denial of having sinned; the person claims innocence and assumes God's anger has passed, but God declares he will judge precisely for this self-deception about one's own sinful state.

Treachery is here an inner failure of fear and fidelity: Judah, though she watched Israel judged for the same sin, did not fear and repeated the betrayal, showing the will's stubborn refusal to be corrected by observed consequences.

Treacherous Judah is judged as worse than faithless Israel; even Israel's apostasy, by comparison, appears more righteous, highlighting that Judah's treachery was a knowing, cynical betrayal that deepened the inner guilt.

Guilt here requires a cognitive act—acknowledging it—because it involves the will's deliberate rebellion and the scattering of devotion among foreign gods; the inner person must own the guilt before restoration is possible.

Transgression is specifically the act of rebelling against God and disobeying his voice; it is presented as something the conscience must acknowledge, linking inner recognition of the will's defiance to the condition for return.

Treachery is likened to a wife abandoning her husband; the inner betrayal is relational and willful, directed against God by the whole house of Israel, emphasizing that the transgression is a breaking of covenantal loyalty from within.

Perverting their way is linked directly to forgetting God; the inner twisting of the will and direction of life leads to spiritual amnesia—the weeping heard shows the resulting distress once the perversion's consequences are felt.

Sinning against God is confessed as spanning from youth to the present day, a lifelong pattern covering both the current generation and their fathers; it produces shame that covers them, placing sin at the root of their dishonor.

Transgressions are many and apostasies great, and together they are the direct cause of the savage judgment descending on the people; the inner accumulation of rebellion brings the outer destruction, showing sin as the engine of doom.
Both houses—Israel and Judah—have been utterly treacherous to God; the totality of the betrayal ('utterly') shows that treachery has fully taken over the inner orientation of the nation toward God.

Sins actively keep good from the people; sin here functions as an inner blockage that turns away divine blessing, showing that sinful conduct has a direct causal effect on the quality of life God withholds.

Iniquities (crimes) turn away God's blessings from the people; the inner moral failures of the community function as a diverting force, redirecting what God would give, connecting inner crime to external deprivation.
Sinning against God is acknowledged as the reason God has given them poisoned water and doom; the people's inner transgression is recognized as the cause of their desperate, hopeless situation.

Treachery is so pervasive among the people that the prophet desires to flee from them; it is associated with adultery and deceit as a communal character, showing treachery as a settled inner disposition that pervades the whole community.

Perverting iniquity is described as a habitual practice that people deliberately teach their tongues; it is a willed, cultivated inner corruption—they weary themselves committing it—showing perversity as chosen and ingrained.

The people's iniquities are those of their forefathers—refusing to hear God's words and serving other gods; returning to these crimes represents a generational pattern of inner covenant-breaking transmitted and embraced anew.

The treacherous thrive while the righteous suffer, creating the inner anguish of the prophet's complaint to God; treachery here is the character of the wicked who prosper, contrasting the inner moral state of the unjust with God's righteousness.

Treachery is here a personal betrayal by family members who speak friendly words while actively pursuing the prophet's destruction; it shows treachery as a hidden inner duplicity—outward kindness masking inward hostility.

Iniquity's greatness is the direct cause of the shame and violence that come upon the people; the inner magnitude of the crime produces proportional humiliation, and the heart that questions its suffering is pointed back to its own wickedness.

Sinning against God is confessed amid a plea for God to act for his own name's sake; sin is acknowledged as having produced the desperate state, yet the appeal is that God's glory transcends the people's sinful record.
Iniquities are personified as witnesses testifying against the people in God's court; the inner crimes are self-accusing, standing as evidence of guilt even as the people plead for mercy.

Sins are remembered by God and punished; the inner choice to wander without restraint—loving to roam rather than seek God—means God will not accept them but will bring accountability for what is stored in his memory.

Iniquity is remembered by God who then punishes the sins; the inner criminal record is held before God's perfect memory, and the willful wandering of feet represents a moral orientation that God notes and judges.

Sinning against God is openly acknowledged alongside the inherited iniquity of the fathers; the corporate confession shows sin as a recognized inner reality that encompasses generations and stands in need of divine action.

Iniquity is acknowledged as inherited from the fathers and confessed as the people's own in the same breath; the inner crime spans generations, making it both a personal and a communal moral burden before God.

Sins are the stated price for which the people's wealth and treasures will be given over as spoil throughout their territory; sin here functions as an inner debt that must be paid through the forfeiture of all external possessions.

Sinning against God is something the people fail to recognize despite facing severe judgment; their question—'what sin have we committed?'—reveals a conscience that has lost awareness of its own sinful inner state.

Sin is presented as something the people cannot identify in themselves even when under judgment; the disconnect between their inner sinful state and their self-perception is itself part of the crisis God is addressing through the prophet.
Iniquity is what provokes God's great evil against the people, yet they are unaware of its nature; the inner crime is real and known to God even as the guilty conscience fails to recognize it.
God's eyes watch all the people's ways and their iniquity is not hidden from him; the inner crime, however concealed from others or from themselves, is fully transparent before God's omniscient gaze.

Sin is paired with iniquity and both are repaid double; the inner offenses are specifically enacted by polluting God's land with idols, showing sin as the inner motivation that produces outward desecration of what belongs to God.

Iniquity and sin together provoke double repayment; the inner crime is evidenced by the outward pollution of God's land with idolatrous abominations, showing the criminal nature of the inner corruption against the holy.

Sin is engraved on the tablet of the heart with an iron pen and diamond point; it is written into the inner person's core—the heart—making it an indelible, structural feature of Judah's inner being rather than a surface-level failing.

Sin is the price for which the people's high places—their centers of idolatrous worship—will cost them their wealth; sin here is tied to the pervasive inner devotion given to false worship throughout all their territory.

Those who turn away from God—degenerating from relationship with the fountain of living water—will be shamed and written in the earth rather than in the book of life; departing from God is the defining act of degeneracy here.

Sin is something that can be blotted out from God's sight, and the prophet prays that it will not be for his enemies; sin is held in God's memory before him and carries a weight that only divine cancellation can remove.

Iniquity is paired with sin in a plea that God not forgive it; both the inner crime and its outward sinful expression are presented as standing before God's sight, deserving judgment rather than mercy in this context.

The iniquity of Babylon and the Chaldeans is what prompts God's punishment after seventy years; their inner crimes accumulate until the appointed time of reckoning, showing iniquity as a stored moral debt that God eventually calls due.

Revolt is here speaking rebellion (false prophecy) against the Lord; the inner defiance is expressed through the mouth and directed against divine authority, and it results in the prophet's death within the year as direct judgment.

Revolt is speaking rebellion against God, and it cuts the speaker off from sharing in the good God will do for his people; the inner defiance expressed in false speech results in exclusion from community and blessing.

Sins are described as flagrant—conspicuous and extreme—and are the direct reason God has struck with an enemy's blow; the inner sinfulness is great in degree and open in expression, warranting severe, merciless punishment.

Guilt is great in magnitude and paired with flagrant sins as the stated cause of the harsh punishment from God; the inner weight of guilt is what makes the blow merciless—its enormity corresponds to the severity of the judgment.

Sin here is described as flagrant and great in magnitude, directly causing incurable pain and divine judgment; the verse frames sin as the interior moral condition that produces outward suffering and makes complaint futile.

Guilt is named as the interior moral weight that is 'great,' explaining why the pain is incurable; it is the accumulated moral debt before God that drives divine punishment and renders the people's cry unanswerable.

Iniquity here is personal and individually owned—each person dies for their own crime, not another's; the verse stresses that the inner moral failure carries direct personal consequences, with no transfer of guilt.

Sin is here the object of divine forgetting—God promises to remember it no more under the new covenant; sin is the standing moral record between the person and God that divine forgiveness erases from memory.
Iniquity is what God promises to forgive under the new covenant, framing it as the moral offense held against the people; its removal is the basis for full, unmediated knowledge of God among all.

Guilt is portrayed as something God repays across generations—the moral weight of fathers is transmitted to children; this shows guilt as a relational and consequential burden with intergenerational reach.

Sinning here is an act caused by idolatrous leadership—Judah was led into the gross abomination of child sacrifice; the verse frames sin as something one can be caused to commit by another's corrupt influence.

Sin here is paired with rebellion as a double moral offense against God, described as producing guilt that requires cleansing and forgiveness; the inner dimension is the defilement that only divine action can purge.

Guilt from sin is the moral contamination God promises to cleanse away completely; the verse presents guilt as a pervasive inner defilement directed against God personally, requiring both cleansing and forgiveness.

Transgression here is paired with sin against God and named as an act of rebellion requiring forgiveness; it represents the willful breach of the covenant relationship that only God's forgiving action can mend.
Sin is listed alongside iniquity as what God will forgive if the people turn from their evil ways; it represents the moral offense held on record before God that repentance makes eligible for pardon.
Iniquity is what God stands ready to forgive contingent on the people's turning from evil; it is the moral crime that blocks divine favor, removable only through repentance and divine pardon.

Iniquity here is the crime for which a king, his offspring, and servants face divine punishment; the guilt is corporate yet traced to leadership, and its consequence is comprehensive disaster brought by God.

Sinning here is framed as doing wrong against another person—Jeremiah protests his innocence, asking what offense he committed against king, servants, or people; it is a relational moral failure he denies having made.

Sinning against the Lord is identified as the direct inner-moral cause of the disaster that befell the people; the verse frames disobedience to God's voice as the defining act of sin whose consequence is inevitable.

Sinning against the Lord is specified as failing to obey his voice and walk in his law; the verse locates the inner failure as willful non-compliance with divine instruction, which directly produces national disaster.

Being guilty is what Israel's enemies claim not to be, justifying their violence by pointing to Israel's sin against God; the verse shows guilt as a moral status assessed relationally—absence of guilt is claimed by those who act against the already-condemned.

Sinning against the Lord—Israel's own offense against their righteous God—is cited by enemies as the moral basis for devouring them; sin here is the inner betrayal of covenantal loyalty that strips away divine protection.

Babylon's sin against the Lord is the stated moral justification for the military assault called against her; sin is here an offense directed upward against God himself, making the sinner deserving of destruction.

Sin in Judah shall be sought and not found in the eschatological era of pardon; the verse envisions sin as a moral record entirely erased by divine forgiveness, leaving the remnant morally clean before God.

Iniquity in Israel shall be sought and none found in that future day; God's pardon removes the moral crime so completely that no trace of it remains, showing iniquity as a record fully dissolved by divine forgiveness.

Punishment here is Babylon's deserved consequence of her iniquity—those inside risk being 'cut off in her punishment'; the verse presents punishment as the inevitable, deadly outcome of accumulated guilt that falls on all within reach.

Treacherous dealing by friends and lovers becomes enemy hostility; the verse reveals treachery as a relational betrayal of trust that converts intimates into opponents, leaving the afflicted completely desolate.

Transgressions here are the multiple offenses for which God himself afflicts Jerusalem, handing her over to enemies; the multiplicity of transgressions is the moral ground that directly produces political and social collapse.

Jerusalem sinned grievously, and the inner result is public shame—her nakedness exposed, her honor reversed to contempt; sin is shown to corrupt the person's standing entirely, producing both social disgrace and inner groaning.

Sin's gravity ('grievously') is measured by its effects: filth, loss of honor, exposed nakedness, and inner groaning; the verse shows sin as a deeply shameful inner condition whose consequences manifest outwardly in humiliation.

Transgressions are depicted as bound into a yoke fastened by God's hand and placed on the neck, sapping strength; the verse frames personal transgressions as having a crushing physical and moral weight that enslaves the person.

Transgressions are the basis on which God dealt harshly with Jerusalem; the speaker appeals for reciprocal judgment on enemies, linking her own transgressions to a faint and grieving heart as their inner emotional toll.

False prophets failed to expose the people's iniquity, preventing repentance and restoration; iniquity here is the hidden moral corruption that needed naming and confronting—concealment of it perpetuated disaster.

God is described as making the sufferer's paths crooked; the verse uses twisting/perverting to portray divine action blocking the person's way, depicting the inner experience of disorientation and blocked progress under judgment.

Sins bring deserved punishment, so complaining about it is presented as unreasonable for a still-living person; the verse grounds sin's consequence in a moral order where suffering for one's own sin is just, not cause for grievance.

Transgression and rebellion are openly confessed together in communal lament; the inner dimension is the willful defiance of God compounded by ongoing divine un-forgiveness, leaving the community bearing its own guilt.

The punishment of Jerusalem is framed as exceeding even Sodom's; the verse uses sin-as-punishment to measure the severity of chastisement, showing the daughter of Zion's moral failure as carrying a heavier penal weight.

The punishment of the daughter of Zion is declared greater than Sodom's; the verse presents punishment as the direct and proportional consequence of iniquity, with Jerusalem's greater suffering implying greater moral culpability.

The sins of prophets and iniquities of priests—specifically shedding innocent blood—are named as the direct cause of Jerusalem's ruin; sin here is a leadership failure of the highest moral and spiritual order.

The iniquities of priests who shed righteous blood are identified as the moral crime that brought about the city's destruction; iniquity here is an active, violent offense against justice, perpetrated by those charged with holiness.

Zion's sins will be uncovered and punished in Edom's case; sin here is presented as something that can be hidden but will ultimately be exposed and punished by God, even as Zion's own punishment is now complete.

Zion's iniquity-punishment is declared accomplished, ending her exile, while Edom's iniquity still awaits punishment; the verse shows iniquity as a moral debt whose full payment brings release, contrasting two peoples at different points in that process.

Fathers sinned and are gone, but the present generation bears their iniquities; sin is shown as an act by one generation whose moral consequences are inherited and carried by those who follow, even the innocent.

The community bears the iniquities of fathers who are now dead; iniquity's crime is shown as transmissible across generations, with the living experiencing its weight though they did not personally commit the original offenses.

The fallen crown of national honor is attributed directly to the community's sinning; sin is the inner-moral cause of collective shame and loss of dignity, linking personal and corporate moral failure to visible disgrace.

Israel is called 'nations of rebels' who have transgressed against God continuously from their fathers to the present day; transgression here is persistent, multigenerational defiance of God's authority, rooted in a rebellious will.

The wicked person dies for his own iniquity when left unwarned; the verse shows iniquity as a personal moral crime carrying a death sentence, and the watchman's silence makes him complicit in that moral-legal outcome.

Even after warning, if the wicked does not turn from his iniquity he dies for it; the verse shows iniquity as a stubborn moral condition—refusal to turn from it is itself evidence of the will's deep entrenchment in wrongdoing.

A righteous person who turns away and sins dies for that sin, with righteous deeds forgotten; the verse shows sin as a fatal turning of the will from righteousness, whose moral weight annuls prior goodness.

Injustice is what a formerly righteous person commits when they turn from righteousness; it is the active moral perversion—opposite of righteousness—that, once committed, brings a divine stumbling block and death.

The righteous person warned not to sin and who heeds the warning shall live; sin here is a potential act of the will that can be averted by warning, showing the person retains moral agency and responsibility to choose rightly.

The prophet is commanded to symbolically bear Israel's punishment by lying on his side; the verse presents punishment as a real, embodied weight placed upon a person, signifying the full gravity of the accumulated iniquity.

Three hundred ninety days of bearing punishment are assigned proportionally to the years of Israel's iniquity; the verse shows punishment as quantifiable and proportional to the duration of the people's moral failure.

The prophet bears Judah's punishment for forty days, one per year; the symbolic act makes the punishment of iniquity physically embodied and temporally measured, showing its weight as real and personally borne.

Iniquity here carries the weight of its own punishment: the people physically rot away, their inner guilt externalized as bodily and communal decay—bread and water withheld, despair shared face to face.

Guilt is implied by the totality of destruction—altars, idols, and works all ruined—suggesting the people's devotion to false worship constitutes a culpable state that God answers with complete desolation.

The iniquity is a criminal weight that drains the person's capacity to sustain life itself; no financial recovery is possible because the person's own crime prevents him from maintaining his existence.

Survivors who escape mourn individually, each person inwardly grieving over his own iniquity—indicating personal, conscious ownership of wrongdoing as an ongoing inner burden in the aftermath of judgment.

Gold and silver, pursued as security, actually functioned as the stumbling block of their iniquity—material greed was the criminal fault that tripped them, rendering their wealth powerless on the day of wrath.
Perversion fills the city as injustice: twisted structures of social order reflecting an inner moral distortion so complete that the people have convinced themselves God neither sees nor cares.
