The guilt of Israel and Judah is declared exceedingly great, manifested outwardly in bloodshed and injustice, rooted in the inner conviction that God has abandoned them and therefore does not hold them accountable.

Iniquity is located explicitly in the heart—idols taken inward—and then placed as a stumbling block before the face, showing the crime originates in the will and then governs the person's entire orientation.

The criminal iniquity is a heart-seated idol devotion that a person positions before his own face, yet he still approaches God's prophet—the inner corruption shapes external religious behavior while remaining self-chosen.

The iniquity again originates in the heart through idol-taking and is then set as a stumbling block before the face; even foreigners in Israel can commit this inner-driven crime of self-separation from God.
Punishment is the iniquity's consequence borne equally by false prophet and deceived inquirer alike, indicating both share the same culpable inner condition and must endure the same penalty.
Transgressions are the repeated acts of going astray and defiling oneself; their cessation is the goal so that a restored covenantal identity—'my people'—can replace the pattern of willful deviation.

The land's sin is an act of will directed against God—sinning against me by acting faithlessly—triggering famine and devastation as divine response, making the sin a relational breach with direct communal consequences.

Unfaithfulness is the active mode of the land's sin against God—acting faithlessly implies a deliberate breach of covenant loyalty, an inner turning away that manifests as treacherous conduct toward the one who owns the relationship.

Unfaithfulness names the disposition behind the land's sin: acting faithlessly describes a settled state of covenant betrayal, the inner condition of disregarding the bond with God, which draws divine judgment of famine and desolation.
Unfaithful conduct is the direct cause of the land's desolation; the inner disposition of betrayal toward God produces irreversible external devastation, with no mention of remedy—only consequence.
Unfaithfulness is the named reason the land is made desolate; it is a settled relational stance of treachery that the people have enacted, and God's response of desolation is its direct penalty.
Sodom's guilt is itemized as pride, excess, and ease coupled with neglect of the poor—showing the inner vice of self-sufficiency and indifference as the heart of their culpable condition.

Jerusalem's sinning exceeds Samaria's in quantity of abominations, making it a comparative moral measure—the more sins committed, the more the perpetrator inadvertently vindicates those who sinned less.
Sin here is quantifiable—half your sins—indicating that the accumulation of abominable acts defines the magnitude of the offense and determines relative moral standing among equally guilty parties.
Sin here is the basis of disgrace and shame; Jerusalem's sins, more abominable than her sisters', result in the inner experience of shame that must be consciously borne as a just consequence.

Unfaithfulness is treachery committed against God personally—the king's covenant-breaking is what draws the divine net and brings him to divine judgment in Babylon, making betrayal the specific inner crime tried.

Treachery against God is the precise charge for which judgment is entered; the unfaithfulness is not merely political disloyalty but a personal offense against God that requires formal divine adjudication.
Sinning is attributed to the individual soul—the soul who sins shall die—establishing that sin is a personal inner act carrying personal mortal consequence, not transferable to another.

Injustice is something the righteous man actively withholds his hand from—a refusal to act unjustly—suggesting injustice is understood as a potential act of the will that must be consciously restrained.

Sin is something the son observes in his father's conduct and consciously chooses not to replicate—perception and deliberate will both engaged—making sin a witnessed pattern that can be broken by inner resolve.

Iniquity is something the hand must be withheld from—it is a temptation to criminal action that the person actively refuses—and non-participation in it is what allows the son to avoid dying for his father's iniquity.
The father's iniquity—extortion and robbery—is his own criminal conduct for which he personally dies; the verse establishes that iniquity as a crime belongs solely to the one who practiced it.

Iniquity is the father's own offense for which only he suffers; the son who lives righteously is exempt, establishing that iniquity as crime is personally borne and not inherited as a punishable liability.

Sinning is attributed exclusively to the individual soul—the soul who sins alone shall die—affirming that sin as an inner act is personally owned and its mortal consequence cannot be displaced onto another person.

Iniquity as crime belongs exclusively to the individual who commits it; it cannot pass from father to son or son to father—each person's criminal wrongdoing remains personally attached and personally judged.

Sin is something a wicked person can turn away from through a decisive act of will—repentance and obedience—showing that sin, however accumulated, is not an immovable inner fate but a course the will can reverse.

Transgressions once committed can be rendered unremembered before God when the person turns to righteousness; the past record of rebellion no longer determines the outcome, erased by the new direction of the will.
Sin committed by the formerly righteous person—having turned from righteousness—is the specific offense for which he dies; it is a conscious act of treachery that eclipses all prior righteous deeds.

Sin here is the act committed by a righteous person who has turned to wickedness; it is named alongside treachery as the double cause of death, indicating that sin committed after righteousness is no less fatal.

Treachery is the inner disposition of unfaithfulness that the formerly righteous person is now guilty of—a turning away that makes him culpable and cancels his prior righteous record, leading to death.

Unfaithfulness is the treachery of which the apostate righteous person is guilty; it represents the inner betrayal of one's prior covenant loyalty to God, for which no past righteousness provides cover.

Injustice is the first act the righteous person does upon turning away from righteousness—it is the initial breach, followed by abominations, indicating that injustice marks the threshold crossing from righteousness to wickedness.

Injustice is the act for which the formerly righteous person dies; the verse repeats the point for emphasis—injustice done after righteousness is its own lethal offense, fully owned by the one who commits it.

Transgressions are not fixed in their consequence when the person considers his ways and turns from them; reflection and repentance—engaging perception and will—dissolve the death-sentence the transgressions would otherwise carry.

Iniquity as crime is personified as ruin: if the people do not repent and turn from transgressions, the iniquity itself becomes the agent of their destruction—a self-destructive inner force made concrete in outcome.
Transgressions are what the people must actively turn from through repentance; they are acts of rebellion that, if not abandoned by a deliberate change of direction, lead directly to ruin.

Transgressing is something committed that must be cast away; the call to a new heart and new spirit follows immediately, linking the act of rebellion directly to the inner renewal needed to replace it.

Transgressions are to be cast away—an act of inner will—and replaced with a new heart and spirit; rebellion is thus not simply behavioral but something seated in the old heart that must be structurally renewed.

Unfaithfulness is equated with blasphemy—dealing treacherously with God is itself a dishonoring of God's name; the inner act of betrayal simultaneously constitutes an act of contempt toward the one betrayed.

The unfaithfulness of the fathers was treacherous dealing with God that amounted to blasphemy; their inner betrayal was not merely moral failure but an offense against God's honor passed down as a pattern.

To transgress is to rebel against God personally; those who do so are purged out—excluded from the restored land—showing that ongoing inner rebellion disqualifies a person from belonging to the covenant community.

Guilt is something brought to remembrance—it resides as a record that God activates at the moment of judgment; the people's sworn oaths cannot suppress it, and its recall leads directly to their capture.

Sins appear in all the people's deeds once their guilt is uncovered; sin is the visible trace of inner corruption that surfaces in conduct, making the person's entire behavioral record a testimony against them.

Guilt is something the people themselves have caused to be remembered through uncovered transgressions and visible sins; making one's guilt memorable is the act that precipitates being seized by divine judgment.
Transgression here is something that has been uncovered and brought to divine remembrance, making Israel visible and accountable; its exposure in all their deeds triggers their capture.
The punishment dimension of iniquity is foregrounded: the wicked prince's day of final reckoning has arrived, meaning his iniquity has now fully ripened into its destined consequence.
False prophetic visions embolden the profane wicked, yet their final punishment still comes at its appointed time; the inner corruption cannot be shielded by deceptive counsel.
Guilt here is incurred specifically through bloodshed and idol-making; it is a legal-moral state that has shortened Jerusalem's days and made her a reproach among the nations.
Sin here is borne as a penalty: Israel must carry the full weight of her sinful idolatry returned upon herself, so that through suffering it she comes to know God.
Iniquity here is a rotting inner corruption that the mourners carry within them; they cannot weep outwardly but waste away internally because of it, groaning among themselves.
Edom's guilt arose from the active will to take revenge on Judah; the word 'grievously offended' intensifies the culpability, showing guilt as the product of deliberate, excessive action.
Injustice is portrayed as something that emerged from within a previously blameless being; it was not original to the cherub but was 'found' in him, marking a moral rupture from within.
Sinning here is the inner turning point: the cherub's inner life was filled with violence through the abundance of trade, and this interior corruption produced the act that led to expulsion.
Injustice in trade profaned the cherub's own sanctuaries; it is depicted as a commercial-moral corruption operating from within, drawing divine fire out from the very midst of the offender.
The multitude of iniquities represents accumulated criminal conduct in trade that desecrated what was holy; the sheer quantity of these crimes provoked a total, consuming divine judgment.
Israel's past iniquity consisted in turning to Egypt for help; the memory of that reliance recalls the inner disposition of unfaith, and Egypt's diminishment will prevent future recurrence.
Iniquities cling to the bones of the fallen warriors in Sheol, marking their identity even in death; their crimes persist as a permanent stain upon them, contrasting with the honored war-dead.
Iniquity here is the individual's own moral condition that brings death; the unwary person perishes 'in his iniquity,' meaning his guilt is the internal ground on which judgment falls.
The wicked person dies 'in his iniquity' when no warning is given; the iniquity is the resident inner offense that is the direct cause of death, not an external sentence imposed arbitrarily.
Sins are described as a crushing weight 'upon us' that causes inner rotting; the people feel them as a living, decaying burden that makes continued life seem impossible.
Transgressions are paired with sins as a joint crushing load; together they produce inner decay and prompt the despairing question of whether life is possible under such accumulated guilt.
Sinning in this verse is a decisive act that breaks the life-sustaining power of prior righteousness; once a righteous person sins, that act overrides his past good standing before God.
Transgression here is the act that nullifies the righteous person's past righteousness; it is the moment of defection that renders previous moral credit unable to save him.
Injustice done by the righteous who trusts his own righteousness erases all his prior good deeds; the verse locates the fatal danger in an act of injustice arising from misplaced self-confidence.
Sin here is something the wicked person can turn from; the verse presents it as a chosen moral direction that can be reversed, with the turn away from sin opening the possibility of life.
Injustice is defined here by its opposite: the wicked who stops practicing it—by restoring pledges and walking in righteous statutes—shall live; abstaining from injustice is the path to life.
Past sins committed by the repentant wicked person are no longer held in divine memory; the verse shows that committed sins can be fully cancelled when the person turns to justice and right.
The sins listed are past committed acts that God chooses not to remember against the repentant; their erasure from divine memory is the basis on which the person is declared to live.
When a righteous person turns to injustice, the act itself becomes lethal; the verse makes clear that injustice is not a minor lapse but a fatal turning of the will that forfeits life.
Punishment here arrives at a predetermined 'final' moment for Israel; Edom exploited that moment of punitive vulnerability, making the time of iniquity's consequence into an occasion for cruelty.
Sinning here describes Israel's past backsliding into idolatry; God promises to save them from it and cleanse them, showing sin as a defilement that requires divine rescue and purification.

Transgressions are listed alongside idols and detestable things as sources of defilement; God promises Israel will no longer defile herself with any of them, presenting transgression as morally polluting.

Unfaithfulness is described as treachery directed personally against God; it caused him to hide his face and hand Israel to enemies, showing that this inner disloyalty severed the divine-human relationship.

God's dealings matched Israel's transgressions; their uncleanness and transgressions are the explicit grounds for the hiding of his face, linking their specific offenses directly to relational rupture.

The treachery practiced against God will be forgotten—not hidden but fully resolved—when Israel dwells securely; the verse presents unfaithfulness as a past shame that restoration will finally eclipse.

Unfaithfulness is described as treachery actively practiced against God; it is remembered as a source of shame, but in the restored future Israel will forget it because it will have been fully dealt with.

To sin here is used in a ritual-purification sense: the altar must be purified and atoned for, treating it as though it carries a defiling condition that blood rites must cleanse before it can be used.

The altar's purification continues with a sin offering goat; sin here operates as a ritual category of impurity requiring sacrificial cleansing, not a moral act by a person but a cultic state of the altar.
Once purifying (sin-cleansing) is finished, unblemished animals are offered; the verse treats sin-purification as a completed prerequisite before acceptable worship can proceed.
The Levites who went astray after idols must bear their own punishment; their willful going far from God is the inner act of apostasy that now returns upon them as inescapable personal consequence.
The Levites became a stumbling block of iniquity by ministering before idols; their culpable service caused others to fall, and they must now bear the punishment that corresponds to that guilt.
Purifying the sanctuary on the first day of the year uses a bull's blood to cleanse from sin; sin is treated as a ritual contaminant on the sacred space requiring annual atoning removal.
Transgression here is the moral cause that hands over the burnt offering and allows truth to be thrown to the ground; it is the interior corruption that gives hostile power its destructive license.
The transgression that makes desolate is the specific act of rebellion that stops the regular offering and desecrates the sanctuary; it is a willful offense against God that brings total ruin.

Transgressors are those who have pressed their rebellion to its absolute limit; the verse frames transgression as a measurable, accumulating quality whose reaching of fullness triggers the rise of a fierce king.

Sinning is the first in a cascade of confessed inner-to-outer failures: sinning, doing wrong, acting wickedly, and rebelling all describe a comprehensive turning of the will away from God's commandments.

To pervert here means to twist or distort righteous conduct; placed between sinning and acting wickedly, it names the inner bending of moral direction away from God's rules as part of total moral failure.

The treachery committed against God is the specific ground of Israel's shame and exile; it is a personal act of betrayal directed against the Lord, producing a just relational rupture that scatters the nation.
Unfaithfulness is named as the reason God drove Israel into distant lands; the shame they bear in every place of exile is the social and relational consequence of their inner betrayal of God.

Sinning against God is confessed as the cause of open shame belonging to all levels of society—kings, princes, and fathers alike; sin here produces a collective disgrace that defines Israel's present condition.

Sinning against God is linked to transgressing his law and refusing to obey his voice; it is the inner act of disobedience that activated the covenantal curse written in the Law of Moses against Israel.
Sinning is confessed alongside doing wickedly in a prayer that grounds the appeal on God's past saving act; the confession acknowledges that Israel's sin is the reason they need renewed mercy.

Sin here is identified as the cause of divine anger resting on Jerusalem; the people's sins and their fathers' iniquities together have made the city a byword, showing sin's corporate, generational, destructive reach.
Sin is confessed in prayer as both personal ('my sin') and communal ('the sin of my people'); the act of confessing it before God is the context in which Daniel presents his plea for the holy city.

Sin is here a collective moral condition requiring decisive termination — the seventy weeks are decreed precisely to bring it to a complete end, implying it is a pervasive inner-communal reality that must be fully extinguished before righteousness can be established.

Transgression is portrayed as a bounded rebellion that the decreed period is appointed to 'finish,' indicating it is a willful communal defiance with a God-determined limit after which it can no longer continue.

Lewdness is a shameful inner disposition that had been hidden but God threatens to expose it publicly before Israel's lovers, showing it as something the unfaithful person conceals yet which invites divine judgment and abandonment.

Sinning here is a compulsive pattern that intensifies proportionally with prosperity — the more they grew, the more they sinned — revealing a will oriented away from God that worsens with blessing rather than improving.

Sin here is treated as a commodity that corrupt priests eagerly consume — they feed on the people's sin offerings, profiting from transgression — showing sin as something that corrupts the whole religious system when leaders exploit it for gain.

Iniquity here is something the priests actively crave — they are greedy for it — revealing that the corrupt inner orientation of the religious leaders is one of appetite and desire directed toward the people's wrongdoing rather than against it.

Guiltiness is here a contagious inner state — Israel's harlotry threatens to infect Judah — so the warning is to avoid association with idolatrous sites so Judah does not incur the same guilty standing before God.

Guilt here is the accumulated weight of Israel's iniquity that causes them to stumble — it is an inner burden that actively undermines their footing, and pride makes it visible on their face even as it trips them.

Acting treacherously here is a covenant-breaking disposition directed against God himself — they have dealt faithlessly with the Lord — expressed outwardly in bearing alien children, indicating deep disloyalty originating in the will.

Guilt is here the inner state that estranges Israel from God — God withdraws until they acknowledge it — showing that recognizing and confessing guilt is the necessary first movement of conscience that opens the way back to God.

Treachery here is covenant-breaking that mirrors Adam's act — they dealt faithlessly with God at a specific place — presenting it as a willful personal betrayal of a relational bond, not mere ritual failure.

Iniquity here is a hidden moral corruption that is exposed when God attempts to heal Israel — it surfaces as deceit, theft, and raiding — showing it as an inner reality that divine scrutiny reveals despite outward concealment.

Rebelling against God is here paired with straying from him — it is willful departure from a relationship — and its inner character is confirmed by the lies they speak against God even as he sought to redeem them.

Transgression here is double rebellion — violating both covenant and law — which has brought divine judgment swooping like a vulture, showing that willful defiance of God's established order provokes immediate and catastrophic consequences.

Sinning here is ritualized and self-reinforcing — altars multiplied for sinning become altars that produce more sinning — showing how institutionalized worship misdirected from God entrenches the sinful inner orientation rather than correcting it.

Sins here are retained in divine memory and will be formally punished — God does not accept their sacrifices but remembers and acts — showing sin as a moral debt that accumulates on record before God and demands reckoning.

Iniquity here is held in God's active memory and leads directly to punitive exile — he will remember their iniquity — showing it as a relational offence that remains present before God until he acts in judgment.

Great iniquity here is paired with great hatred as the underlying cause of the coming days of punishment and recompense, revealing it as an intense, spiritually corrupting inner reality that triggers God's retributive response.

Sins here are subject to divine punitive memory — he will punish their sins — placed alongside deep self-corruption, indicating that sin is a settled inner defilement whose consequences God will inevitably enforce.

Iniquity here is held in God's memory as the basis for punishment — he will remember their iniquity — showing it as a recorded inner moral failure that persists before God and cannot be forgotten without consequence.

Guilt here flows from a false heart — deceitful inner disposition produces guilt that must be borne — making the connection explicit: corrupt inner character generates a weight of guilt that the people are forced to carry.

Sin here is identified with idolatrous high places — the sin of Israel that shall be destroyed — showing that Israel's sin has taken institutional form in worship sites, and its destruction will be so complete they cry for the hills to bury them.

Sinning here is a persistent historical pattern extending from Gibeah — they have sinned and continued — showing a wilful, unbroken inner disposition of transgression that has persisted across generations and will finally be overtaken by war.

Iniquity here is the moral characteristic of the 'unjust' against whom war comes — it is an established condition of those at Gibeah — showing it as a defining inner quality that makes a people legitimate targets of military judgment.

Guilt here is described as 'double,' a compounded moral burden that binds the people and causes nations to be gathered against them in discipline, showing guilt as an accumulating inner weight that becomes the ground of corporate punishment.

Injustice here is the bitter harvest of a morally corrupted will — they plowed iniquity and reaped injustice — showing it as the inevitable fruit of inner choices, compounded by self-trust rather than reliance on God.

Sin here is something Ephraim self-deceptively denies — his wealth convinces him no sin or iniquity can be found in him — revealing sin as a moral reality that prosperity blinds the conscience to, though it is still present.

Iniquity here is denied by the wealthy Ephraim who claims no iniquity can be found in him, showing it as something the self-deceived conscience suppresses even while it remains a real condition before God.

Incurring guilt through Baal is here the specific act that undid Ephraim's former exaltation and brought death — guilt is the lethal consequence of idolatrous allegiance that destroys what once flourished.

Sinning here is an escalating compulsion — they sin more and more — producing ever more elaborate idolatry including human sacrifice, showing that unrestrained sinful inner orientation accelerates and deepens its own corruption over time.

Sin here is kept in store like a sealed record — it is not forgotten or released but held in divine safekeeping — showing sin as a treasured moral debt against Ephraim that God retains for future reckoning.

Iniquity here is bound up and sealed — preserved in divine record against Ephraim — showing it as a fixed inner moral reality that cannot be dissolved or ignored, awaiting the moment of God's judgment.

Guilt here is borne by Samaria as the consequence of willful rebellion against God, and its bearing is expressed in catastrophic communal violence — the guilt that accumulates through defiance produces a matching severity of punishment.

Iniquity here is the cause of Israel's stumbling and fall — they have stumbled because of their iniquity — showing it as an inner moral condition that destabilizes and trips the person, making return to God the only remedy.

Iniquity here is the specific object of penitent prayer — 'take away all iniquity' — showing that the returning conscience recognizes its iniquity as a burden it cannot remove itself but must ask God to lift completely.

Transgressors here are those who stumble in the Lord's right ways, contrasted with the upright who walk in them — their inner disposition is one of resistance to God's order, causing them to trip on the very path that sustains others.

Transgressions here are accumulated offences of Damascus — specifically brutal military cruelty — that cross a threshold of divine tolerance, showing transgression as a countable moral excess that triggers irreversible divine judgment.

Transgressions here are Gaza's accumulated crimes of wholesale slave trading, which breach the threshold of divine forbearance — showing transgression as morally outrageous actions against persons that God will not overlook indefinitely.

Transgressions here are Tyre's multiple violations, specifically delivering a whole people to Edom and disregarding a covenant of brotherhood — showing transgression as betrayal of relational obligation that God counts and holds against the offender.

Transgressions here are Edom's accumulated acts of ruthless, perpetually sustained rage against his brother — pursued with sword and unrelenting wrath — showing transgression rooted in an inner hatred that is chronic, not momentary.

Transgressions here are the Ammonites' savage atrocities against pregnant women to expand territory — showing transgression as crimes of calculated brutality driven by territorial ambition, which exhaust divine patience and invite judgment.

Transgressions here are Moab's desecration of the dead — burning the king of Edom's bones to lime — showing transgression as a deeply dishonouring act against human dignity that provokes God's judgment even between foreign nations.

Transgressions here are Judah's rejection of God's law and statutes, compounded by following inherited lies — showing transgression as a wilful inner turning away from divine instruction that the conscience has been misled into sustaining across generations.

Transgressions here are Israel's exploitation of the vulnerable — selling the righteous for silver and the poor for sandals — showing transgression as a morally callous inner disposition that monetizes human dignity without restraint.

Iniquities here are the basis on which God will punish the people he uniquely knew — intimate covenant knowledge intensifies rather than excuses accountability — showing iniquity as a betrayal of the most privileged relational standing.

Transgressions here are the specific grounds on which God will punish Israel on a set day, with the altars of Bethel destroyed as a consequence — showing transgression as the juridical basis for God's measured act of judgment.

Transgressing here is ironically commanded at the very religious sites Israel frequents — 'come to Bethel and transgress' — exposing that their worship is itself rebellion, showing transgression as an inner defiance disguised as devotion.

Sins here are numerous, great in quantity, and specified as oppression of the righteous, bribery, and injustice to the needy — God knows them all — showing sin as a persistent, measurable pattern of exploitative inner moral failure.

Transgressions here are many and known to God — paired with great sins that include bribery and oppression — showing transgression as a quantifiable record of willful wrongdoing that God tracks even when the people may not acknowledge it.

The Guilt of Samaria is here the idol invoked in oaths — it is the false god they swear by — showing guiltiness as a defining identity marker of the idolatrous community whose devotion to a shameful object will lead directly to their fall.
The sinful kingdom is marked by collective moral corruption that draws God's direct gaze and warrants total destruction, though a remnant is spared by grace.
These sinners are characterized by self-deceived presumption—their inner conviction that disaster cannot reach them leads them to deny accountability and face death by the sword.
Sin here is institutionalized in the very capitals of Israel and Judah—Samaria and Jerusalem—making it the defining moral reality of entire nations, not merely individual acts.
Transgression is embodied in the political and religious centers of Jacob itself, showing that deliberate rebellion against God has become structural in the nation's identity.
Sin is portrayed as originating in Lachish and spreading like a contagion to Zion, showing it has a traceable beginning point that corrupts outward from its source.
Transgressions of Israel are said to be found 'in' Lachish, locating rebellion as a resident quality within the city that then infects the daughter of Zion.
Destruction here is an active moral force—the uncleanness of the land itself destroys grievously, making the place uninhabitable and unfit for rest.
Sin is the specific charge the Spirit-empowered prophet is commissioned to name and declare to Israel, framing it as a reality that must be openly confronted rather than concealed.
Transgression is what the prophet, filled with divine power and justice, is sent to declare to Jacob—it is a concrete accusation requiring Spirit-backed courage to announce.
Sin is located in the soul itself ('sin of my soul'), framing it as a deep inner guilt for which the speaker desperately seeks sufficient atonement, even at the cost of one's firstborn.
Transgression is personal and weighty enough that the speaker imagines sacrificing his firstborn child to atone for it, revealing a sense of profound personal guilt before God.
Sins are the direct cause of God's grievous blow of judgment, establishing them as the moral ground that produces devastating outward consequences of desolation.

Sinning against the Lord is personally owned and confessed by the speaker as the reason for bearing divine indignation, showing a conscience that accepts accountability while trusting in God's ultimate vindication.
Iniquity is a burden requiring divine pardon—God's incomparable nature is displayed precisely in his willingness to lift and forgive the iniquity of his people out of steadfast love.
Transgression is something God 'passes over' for the remnant—not ignoring it but actively choosing not to hold it against them, rooted in his delight in steadfast love.
Sins are cast into the depths of the sea by God—completely removed from the people, showing total divine disposal of the guilt that had characterized them.
Iniquities are things God treads underfoot—crushed and subdued—as an act of compassion, depicting them as oppressive forces that God decisively overcomes on behalf of his people.
Guilt here is the inner moral state of those whose self-reliance becomes idolatry—they make their own military might their god, and that act of self-deification constitutes their guilt.
Treacherous persons are those who swallow up the righteous while God looks on silently—their inner character is marked by betrayal of justice, and the prophet finds God's tolerance of them troubling.
Wine acts as a treacherous agent that deceives from within—its betrayal is linked to the arrogant, insatiable inner greed that never rests and consumes entire nations.
Sinning here is the act of devising shame through violent exploitation—the one who cuts off many peoples has forfeited their own life, showing sin carries self-destructive consequence.
Injustice is the moral foundation of a city built by violence—it is a structural wrong embedded in the city's origin, calling down a woe from God.
Sinning against the Lord is the stated cause of the coming blindness and bloody destruction of mankind, directly linking inner moral failure to catastrophic outward judgment.
Injustice is what God does not do—contrasting his consistent righteous justice each morning with the unjust person who feels no shame, exposing injustice as a conscience-dulled inner disposition.
The unjust person is defined here by the absence of shame—no internal moral response checks their conduct, marking them as inwardly insensible to wrong in contrast to God's righteousness.

Transgression is expressed through deeds of proud rebellion against God; on the day of restoration those deeds will no longer bring shame because the proudly exultant—the source of that rebellion—will be removed.
Injustice is paired with lying and deceit as behaviors the purified remnant will no longer practice—its absence defines the restored inner integrity of those who are left.

Iniquity clings to Joshua as filthy garments—a visible moral contamination that God actively removes, replacing it with pure vestments, showing iniquity as something that defiles and must be divinely taken away.
The iniquity of the whole land is something God will remove in a single day through the engraved stone set before Joshua, showing it as a collective moral debt resolved by a decisive divine act.

Being guilty ('go unpunished' translating a.sham) is avoided by the buyers who slaughter their flock without consequence—guilt is treated as irrelevant by those who profit, exposing a moral indifference to wrongdoing.
Sin is a moral impurity from which the house of David needs cleansing—a fountain is opened specifically to purify them, depicting sin as a defilement requiring an external divine remedy.
Sin as punishment falls on Egypt and all nations who refuse to observe the Feast of Booths, showing that sin here functions as the divinely imposed consequence of willful non-compliance.

Wickedness is a settled national identity God assigns to Edom—they are 'the wicked country,' a people against whom God's anger is permanent, showing wickedness as a defining corporate moral character.

Injustice (wrong) is absent from the faithful priest's lips—his mouth holds truth and his walk is upright, making his integrity the direct structural opposite of injustice as speech-and-conduct failure.
Iniquity is a direction people travel in, from which the faithful priest actively turned many away—framing it as a moral path that can be redirected through truthful instruction.
Acting treacherously is equated with being faithless to one another within the covenant community—it is a relational betrayal rooted in ignoring the shared fatherhood of God.
Faithlessness in Judah is expressed through profaning God's sanctuary and marrying a foreign god's daughter—treachery here is a covenantal and spiritual betrayal committed at the corporate level.
Acting treacherously is directed against the wife of one's youth—a personal covenant betrayal witnessed by God himself, making faithlessness here a violation of the most intimate human bond.
Faithlessness to the wife of one's youth is a matter requiring vigilance in one's spirit—the call to guard the spirit frames treachery as an inner disposition that must be actively resisted.
Faithlessness is linked to divorce and covered by violence—it is an act of the will and spirit that God directly opposes, requiring self-vigilance in the spirit to prevent.

Wickedness here is the condition of those who prosper while testing God—the speaker bitterly observes that evildoers escape consequence, revealing a crisis of moral order where wickedness appears unpunished.

Wickedness and arrogance together define those who will be consumed like stubble on the coming day—evildoers are identified by their inner disposition of proud defiance that ends in complete destruction.
Sins are the defining bondage from which Jesus comes to save his people—his very name and mission are shaped by the need to rescue humanity from this inner moral and spiritual condition.
Sins are confessed openly at baptism—the act of public verbal acknowledgment implies inner moral awareness and willingness to own one's wrongdoing before God as a prerequisite for repentance.

The unjust receive the same sun and rain as the just from God—the term marks them as morally deficient persons whom God nonetheless sustains in common grace, defining unjust as a moral condition, not a disqualification from creation's benefits.
Sins are spoken to the paralytic's inner person by Jesus—'your sins are forgiven' addresses the soul directly, suggesting sin as a spiritual burden that Jesus has authority to lift.
Sins here are something that can be spoken as forgiven—Jesus frames their forgiveness as equivalent in difficulty and authority to commanding physical healing, linking sin's removal to divine power.
Sins are what the Son of Man has authority to forgive on earth—this authority is explicitly demonstrated through physical healing, showing sin as a condition requiring divine jurisdiction to remove.

Sinners are a recognized social and moral category who gather around Jesus at table—their association with tax collectors marks them as those whose inner moral standing excludes them from respectable society but not from Jesus.
The Pharisees use 'sinners' as a social-moral label marking people whose inner character is seen as defiled, making table fellowship with them scandalous in their view.
Jesus identifies sinners as those who need his call, contrasting them with the self-styled righteous; their inner condition is one of need rather than self-sufficiency.
Jesus is labeled a friend of sinners by critics who view such association as morally compromising; sinners here are those whose lifestyle disqualifies them from respectable standing.

Sin here is presented as something forgivable when committed against people or in general, establishing it as an offense that divine mercy can cover, unlike the uniquely unforgivable blasphemy against the Spirit.
Blasphemy in general is forgivable, but blasphemy specifically directed against the Holy Spirit crosses an inner boundary into permanent, irreversible guilt before God.
Transgressing here means stepping over an inherited communal boundary (the elders' tradition), framing the disciples' will as defiant of established religious authority.

Jesus turns the charge back, showing that transgression can be directed against God's own commandment when human tradition is elevated above it, revealing a deeper and more culpable willful crossing of divine law.
Slander (blasphemy) is listed among evils that originate inside the heart, locating its source in the inner moral center of the person rather than in external ritual pollution.
Sinning here is a relational act directed against a specific person (a brother), making the inner-being dimension one of interpersonal wrong that must be addressed by direct, private confrontation.
Peter frames sin as a repeated interpersonal offense — 'sin against me' — asking how the will to forgive should respond to the will that keeps offending.
Sin here is presented as a corporate burden requiring atoning blood; Jesus' shed blood establishes a covenant whose purpose is the forgiveness of the accumulated guilt of many.
Sinners here are those who will enact the betrayal and arrest of Jesus, marking them as agents of lawless evil into whose power the Son of Man is delivered.
The high priest declares Jesus' claim blasphemy — a verbal act he treats as a capital offense against God — using it to close the trial and secure a death sentence.
Judas confesses inwardly to having sinned by a specific act — betraying innocent blood — showing conscience naming a concrete, blameworthy moral failure.
Sin here is something people turn from through repentance, requiring external rite (baptism) tied to inner turning, and whose forgiveness is the goal of John's proclamation.
People approach baptism confessing their sins — an inner act of acknowledgment where the will openly owns its offenses before God as part of receiving forgiveness.
Jesus declares the paralytic's sins forgiven in response to faith, linking inner moral guilt to divine authority and showing sin as a burden that can be released by Jesus' word.
The scribes treat sin as something only God can forgive, so Jesus claiming to forgive it strikes them as arrogant intrusion into divine prerogative — which is why they call it blasphemy.

The scribes charge Jesus with blasphemy because he claims to forgive sins, treating this as a usurpation of divine authority — the inner offense is presumptuous speech that trespasses on God's exclusive role.
Jesus frames sin's forgiveness as an inner, invisible reality by contrasting it with the visible, verifiable command to rise and walk, suggesting forgiven sin is a hidden but real transformation.
The Son of Man's authority to forgive sins on earth is the inner-being claim being demonstrated; sin is depicted as something requiring authoritative release, not merely human absolution.

Sinners are those who recline with Jesus at table — socially and morally marginalized people whose inner condition is viewed by the religious establishment as disqualifying for association with the holy.
The scribes of the Pharisees view eating with sinners as morally contaminating, treating their sinful status as an inner-character defect that should be avoided rather than engaged.
Jesus likens sinners to the sick who need a physician, framing their sinful condition as an inner moral illness that he has come specifically to address and heal.
All sins and blasphemies spoken by people fall within the scope of divine forgiveness, indicating no ordinary moral or verbal failing places a person permanently beyond God's mercy.
Whatever blasphemies people utter are included in the category of forgivable offenses, setting up the contrast with the single unforgivable blasphemy against the Holy Spirit in the next verse.
Blasphemy against the Holy Spirit constitutes an eternal sin — a state of guilt with no exit — suggesting an inner condition of such deep opposition to the Spirit that forgiveness is never obtained.
Slander (blasphemia) appears in a list of inner-generated evils, showing it originates from the heart's moral corruption and expresses itself outwardly as destructive speech against others.
The generation is described as sinful, linking the corporate moral character of the age to the shame its members feel about Jesus; sinfulness here is a defining inner trait of the entire era.
The Son of Man is betrayed into the hands of sinners — those whose inner moral character places them in opposition to the holy, making them instruments of lawless violence against the innocent.
The council declares Jesus' speech blasphemy and unanimously condemns him to death, treating the word as a capital offense — the inner act of arrogating divine status is judged fatal.
Forgiveness of sins is the content of the salvation knowledge Zechariah's prophecy promises; sin here is the burden from which God's people need deliverance to receive salvation.
John's baptism is a rite of repentance aimed at the forgiveness of sins, framing sin as the inner moral debt that must be acknowledged and turned from before salvation arrives.
Peter's confession — 'I am a sinful man' — is a spontaneous inner recognition of his own moral unworthiness in the presence of Jesus' divine power, driving him to prostrate himself in awe.
Jesus pronounces the paralyzed man's sins forgiven in response to observed faith, treating sin as a moral debt that divine authority can simply remit, independent of any sacrifice or legal process.
The question 'who can forgive sins but God alone?' treats sin as an offense against God whose cancellation belongs exclusively to him, making Jesus' claim an implied assertion of divine identity.

The scribes and Pharisees hear Jesus' forgiveness declaration as blasphemy — a usurpation of God's exclusive prerogative — showing that the inner offense of blasphemy is defined here as improper claim to divine authority.
Jesus contrasts declaring sins forgiven with commanding physical healing, positioning the forgiveness of sin as an invisible inner transaction that is harder to verify than a visible bodily cure.
The healing miracle is performed to demonstrate that the Son of Man possesses real authority to forgive sins on earth — sin is the deeper problem; physical paralysis is its visible illustration.
Pharisees object to Jesus eating with sinners, treating such persons' moral status as a contaminant that disqualifies them from shared table fellowship with the religiously observant.
Jesus explicitly names sinners — not the self-righteous — as his target audience, framing their sinful condition as the very need that draws him and the ground for calling them to repentance.

Jesus uses sinners' natural reciprocal love as a baseline moral minimum, implying that loving only one's friends requires no inner transformation and is no different from what sinful people already do.

Sinners do good to those who do good to them — a morally limited reciprocity that stays within self-interest; the point is that sinfulness does not mean total depravity but rather a restricted, self-centered goodness.
Even sinners lend expecting return — operating by calculated self-interest; their inner orientation is shown as fundamentally transactional rather than graciously other-centered.
Critics use 'friend of sinners' as a slur against Jesus, treating the sinful person's inner moral condition as something that morally contaminates those who associate with them.
The woman is identified by the city as 'a sinner,' a known moral status that defines her public identity and makes her approach to Jesus in a Pharisee's house socially scandalous.

The Pharisee privately concludes that a prophet would know this woman is a sinner and therefore refuse her touch; her sinful inner character is seen as a moral contagion a holy man should detect and avoid.

The woman's many sins are forgiven because she loved much — Jesus links the depth of inner gratitude and love to the magnitude of forgiveness received, making her sins the backdrop that measures the grace given.
Jesus directly declares the woman's sins forgiven, addressing her inner moral burden personally and authoritatively, releasing her from the guilt that had defined her social and spiritual identity.
Bystanders question who Jesus is that he forgives sins, registering that sin-forgiveness is recognized as an exclusively divine act, making his doing it a claim to authority they cannot categorize.
Sin here is something accumulated against God that requires divine forgiveness, paired with the disciples' own willingness to forgive debtors, linking inner guilt with relational obligation.
The moth's destroying of earthly wealth illustrates the corruptibility of material possessions, contrasted with heavenly treasure that is immune to such decay.
Jesus challenges the assumption that greater suffering signals greater sinfulness, rejecting a direct moral-causal link between personal sin and catastrophe.
Those publicly classified as sinners are drawn toward Jesus to hear him, showing their inner openness to teaching despite their social-moral stigma.
The Pharisees' complaint that Jesus receives sinners and eats with them frames such persons as morally disqualified from fellowship, revealing a judgment based on perceived inner corruption.
The sinner who repents triggers greater heavenly joy than the righteous who need no repentance, placing the inner act of turning as the decisive moral moment.
A single sinner's repentance produces joy among the angels, affirming that the inner turning of a morally wayward person has cosmic significance.
The prodigal's planned confession—'I have sinned against heaven and before you'—shows sin understood as a dual offense against God and father, recognized by a newly awakened conscience.
The son's spoken confession—'I have sinned against heaven and before you'—enacts genuine inner repentance; his admission of unworthiness shows the self-knowledge sin has produced.
Dishonesty in small matters reveals a fixed character trait—the unjust person is unjust across all scales, showing injustice as a stable inner disposition rather than situational behavior.
Unfaithfulness with 'unrighteous wealth' disqualifies a person from being trusted with true riches, making inner dishonesty a condition that forfeits greater stewardship.
Sinning against a brother calls for rebuke and, upon repentance, forgiveness—framing sin as a relational breach between persons that conscience and will must address.

Repeated sinning against another—even seven times in a day—does not exhaust the obligation to forgive if the offender truly repents, showing sin's relational damage as resolvable through genuine inner turning.
The Pharisee lists 'unjust' alongside extortioners and adulterers as a category he thanks God he does not belong to, using injustice as a marker of inner moral corruption.
The tax collector's plea—'be merciful to me, a sinner'—shows sinfulness experienced as a deep inner unworthiness that drives him to prostrate, eyes-down prayer rather than self-justification.
The crowd's grumbling that Jesus lodged with 'a sinner' treats Zacchaeus's moral status as a social and spiritual disqualification that should exclude him from honorable company.
Jesus being 'delivered into the hands of sinful men' identifies his crucifiers by their inner moral character—those whose sinfulness makes them agents of unjust violence.
Sin here is the condition requiring both repentance and forgiveness, and proclamation of that forgiveness in Jesus's name is the universal remedy to be declared to all nations.
Sin is portrayed as a collective burden weighing on the whole world that only the Lamb of God can lift away, framing it as a universal human condition requiring divine removal.
Jesus warns the healed man to sin no more lest something worse befall him, implying that continued sinning exposes the person to escalating consequences beyond physical illness.
Jesus withholds condemnation but commands the woman to sin no more, showing sin as a pattern of conduct the will must forsake in light of mercy received.
Dying 'in your sin' means sin is an unresolved inner condition that determines one's final state and separation from where Jesus goes—sin here is a lethal spiritual location.
Dying in one's sins is tied directly to unbelief in Jesus as 'I am'; sin's fatal hold cannot be broken apart from that belief, linking inner faith to liberation from sin's power.
Practicing sin makes one a slave to sin—Jesus describes sin as a dominating inner master that subjugates the will of the one who habitually commits it.
Jesus challenges his opponents to convict him of sin, implying his own inner life and conduct are wholly without it—sin's absence in him is the ground of his credibility.
The disciples assume someone's sin caused the man's blindness—either his own or his parents'—treating sinning as a cause with physical, even prenatal, consequences.
Jesus denies that either the man's or his parents' sinning caused his blindness, breaking the assumed sin-suffering causal link and redirecting to God's purpose.

Some Pharisees label Jesus a sinner because he broke the Sabbath, while others question how a sinner could do such signs—sinfulness is contested as an inner status that would disqualify divine action.
The authorities declare Jesus a sinner with certainty, using that moral verdict to discredit him—inner sinfulness is treated as an established fact that negates any claim to divine authority.
The healed man honestly admits he cannot determine whether Jesus is a sinner, but insists on the undeniable fact of his own healing—distinguishing inner moral status from observable effect.
The principle stated is that God does not hear sinners but does hear worshipers who do his will—inner sinfulness cuts off the sinner from divine responsiveness, while obedience opens it.
Being 'born in utter sin' is hurled as an accusation to disqualify the healed man from teaching, framing sin as a condition present from birth that corrupts one's standing entirely.
Claiming to see while remaining blind to Jesus constitutes guilt that stays—sin here is tied to a willful refusal of knowledge, making self-declared perception the very ground of culpability.
The Jews charge Jesus with blasphemy specifically because he, a man, claims to be God—blasphemy here is the inner presumption of claiming divine identity, treated as an offense deserving death.
Jesus's coming and speaking removed any excuse for unbelief—sin now attaches to those who heard him because they can no longer plead ignorance; knowledge creates moral accountability.
Witnessing Jesus's unique works and still hating him makes their sin inescapable—sin here is willful rejection of unmistakable evidence, transforming hatred of Jesus into guilt before God.
The Spirit's coming role includes convicting the world concerning sin—sin is a condition the world does not self-recognize and requires an external divine agent to expose.
The Spirit convicts of sin specifically because the world does not believe in Jesus—unbelief is identified as the root sin, making disbelief the inner disposition that defines culpability.
Jesus distinguishes degrees of sin—the one who handed him over bears greater sin than Pilate—showing sin as morally gradated by the degree of knowledge and deliberate intent involved.
Sin is a condition that can be forgiven or retained by those commissioned by Jesus, implying it is a relational debt with God that requires authoritative absolution or remains bound.
Judas 'turned aside' from his apostolic place—his transgression was an inner defection of will from a sacred charge, departing to 'his own place' as the outcome of that inner betrayal.
Sins here are the accumulated offenses requiring forgiveness, which repentance and baptism in Jesus's name address—sin is the inner condition that must be resolved before the Spirit is received.
Sins are described as things that can be 'blotted out' upon repentance and turning—framing them as a recorded inner debt that divine action can fully erase.
Forgiveness of sins is paired with the gift of repentance, both granted through the exalted Jesus—sin is the condition that makes repentance necessary and whose removal is the Savior's purpose.
False witnesses claim Stephen spoke blasphemous words against Moses and God—blasphemy is framed as verbal aggression against sacred authorities, used as a charge to discredit his inner loyalty.
Stephen's dying prayer asks God not to hold this sin against his killers—sin is a moral debt that can be charged or released, and Stephen's intercession mirrors Christ's forgiving posture.
Belief in Jesus brings forgiveness of sins through his name—sin is a universal human condition the prophets anticipated would be resolved, available to everyone who believes.
Forgiveness of sins is proclaimed through this man—Jesus—as the central content of the gospel message, framing sin as the primary problem the gospel addresses in the inner life of hearers.
Gallio distinguishes a 'crime' or 'vicious wrongdoing' as the kind of offense that would justify legal intervention, framing adikēma as an actionable moral-legal violation of public order.
Apostasy here is the accusation that Paul teaches Jews to forsake Moses—to abandon circumcision and ancestral customs—framing it as an inner defection from covenantal identity.

Sins are presented as an inner defilement clinging to the person that must be actively washed away through baptism and calling on Christ's name, implying they are a moral stain requiring cleansing before one can stand before God.

The unjust are contrasted with the just as a distinct moral category of persons, both of whom will face resurrection and thus ultimate accountability, indicating the unjust are those whose conduct has been wrongful before God.

Paul challenges his accusers to name a specific wrongdoing they found in him before the council, framing crime as a demonstrable act of injustice that requires evidence and can be adjudicated in a legal setting.

Paul denies having committed any offense against Jewish law, the temple, or Caesar, framing sinning as an act of violation against recognized religious and civil authorities that would constitute a punishable wrong.

Sins are linked to spiritual blindness, darkness, and Satan's power; forgiveness of sins is the inner liberation that comes when a person turns to God, positioning sin as the defining bond holding the soul in darkness and away from sanctification.

Sinning is presented as a universal human act with inevitable judicial consequences — perishing or being judged — whether one had the law or not, showing it is an inner moral reality that operates independently of having explicit legal knowledge.

Breaking the law while boasting in it is identified as a direct dishonoring of God, showing that transgression is a willful violation that contradicts one's professed allegiance and corrupts the inner integrity of the one who claims to honor God.

Breaking the law nullifies the outward sign of circumcision, making the transgressor effectively uncircumcised; the inner reality of willful law-breaking undoes religious identity and standing before God.

The law-breaker who possesses written code and circumcision will be condemned by the uncircumcised who actually keeps the law, showing that transgressing the law is an inner failure of the will that overrides all outward religious advantage.

Paul raises the rhetorical question of whether God is unjust to inflict wrath, immediately qualifying it as a merely human way of reasoning; the unjust quality here is tested against God's perfect righteousness as its opposite standard.

The sinner is one condemned even when their lie paradoxically served to magnify God's truth; being a sinner here is an inner moral status that makes one liable to judgment regardless of the outcome that flows from one's act.

Being 'under sin' describes a universal condition of moral bondage encompassing all humanity — both Jews and Greeks — portraying sin as a dominating power that holds the entire person in subjection rather than merely a series of discrete acts.

The law's function is to produce knowledge of sin in the mind and conscience, meaning sin is a reality that must be cognitively recognized before it can be addressed; it is not self-evident without a standard to reveal it.

All having sinned is the universal inner-moral condition that results in falling short of God's glory, linking the act of sinning directly to a deficiency in reflecting the divine nature that was humanity's intended end.

Former sins were passed over in God's forbearance but required a propitiation — Christ's blood — to demonstrate God's righteousness; the sins here are specific past offenses that accumulated a moral debt demanding a just resolution.

Sins are depicted as deeds that cover and defile the person, requiring divine forgiveness and covering to restore blessing; the image of being 'covered' implies sins as a visible moral stain that must be hidden from God's judgment.

The blessed condition is when the Lord does not count sin against a person, presenting sin as a moral debt on a divine ledger; not counting it is an act of gracious non-imputation rather than the sin's non-existence.

Transgression is defined here as law-violation that cannot exist without a law to violate; it is thus a relational and legal concept — the willful crossing of a known boundary — that only becomes possible when a command has been given.

Christ was delivered up specifically on account of human trespasses, showing that trespasses are moral failures creating a debt that required the ultimate substitutionary cost of the Son's death to satisfy and secure justification.

Being a sinner is the moral condition Christ died for — 'while we were still sinners' — meaning Christ's love was demonstrated precisely when humanity was in its worst inner state, with no mitigating righteousness to commend them.

All humanity is said to have sinned as a result of Adam's original act, and death spread universally on that basis; sinning is here both an inherited condition and a personal participation, linking individual acts to a corporate human guilt.

Sin entered the world through one man as an invading power that brought death in its wake and spread universally through all humanity, depicting sin as a destructive force with cosmic reach that corrupts and kills the whole person.

Sin existed as a reality in the world even before the law gave it formal definition; it is not legally counted without law, yet its presence and lethal effect are real, showing sin is an ontological condition prior to any explicit moral code.

Death reigned even over those whose sinning was not the same kind as Adam's explicit transgression of a direct command, showing that sinning produces mortal consequences even in forms that differ from the original paradigmatic act.

Adam's transgression is the paradigm against which other forms of sinning are measured — a specific, conscious violation of a direct divine command that set a pattern for subsequent condemnation of all humanity.

One man's trespass brought death to many, yet the free gift of grace far surpasses it; the trespass here is a single moral misstep with catastrophic communal consequences, setting the scale of human fallenness against which grace is measured.

One man's sin triggered judgment and condemnation, while the free gift reversed many trespasses into justification; the single act of sinning is presented as a legal and moral event that altered the standing of all before God.

One trespass brought condemnation while many trespasses are overcome by the free gift of justification; trespass here is a moral fault that generates a legal verdict of condemnation requiring an extraordinary counter-act of grace to reverse.

Because of one man's trespass death itself reigned as a tyrant; the trespass is thus a foundational moral failure in one person that unleashed a reign of death over all, underscoring the catastrophic inner and cosmic reach of a single act of disobedience.

One trespass led to universal condemnation for all men, directly mirrored by one act of righteousness leading to justification for all; the trespass is the moral pivot point that determined the condemned status of the entire human race.

Many were constituted sinners through one man's disobedience — not by their individual choice but by their participation in Adam — showing that being a sinner is an imputed inner status arising from a federal representative act of disobedience.

Sin's increase when the law came in is met by an even greater increase of grace; sin is portrayed here as a quantifiable moral reality whose multiplication does not outpace divine grace, yet it is a genuine proliferating power demanding grace's response.

The law entered specifically to increase the trespass, giving explicit legal definition to what was already morally present; trespass is thus the concrete instance of law-violation that the law multiplies by naming and prohibiting specific transgressions.

Sin is described as having 'reigned in death,' portraying it as a tyrannical ruling power whose dominion over the person leads to death; grace's counter-reign through righteousness shows sin's reign is a competing authority within human existence.

The rhetorical question asks whether one should continue in sin to magnify grace; sin is here treated as a behavioral domain in which a person can persist or not, suggesting it is an ongoing pattern of life under a power one can choose to remain in.

Believers are described as having died to sin, making continued living in it a contradiction; sin is a sphere of existence or a ruling domain one can be dead to, such that ongoing life in it is incompatible with the death-and-resurrection identity of the believer.

The 'body of sin' is the old self's bodily existence oriented toward sin as master; crucifying the old self aims at destroying sin's bodily dominion so that the person is no longer enslaved to it, showing sin as a master that colonizes the body.

Death itself is presented as the mechanism of liberation from sin's power, showing sin as a master or enslavement from which one can only be freed by dying; it holds the living person in bondage that only dissolution of the old self can break.

Christ died to sin 'once for all,' meaning his death was a definitive termination of any relationship to sin's domain; sin is the sphere of mortal existence he exited permanently, establishing the pattern for the believer's own death to it.

Believers are to reckon themselves as dead to sin in their minds and wills, indicating that dying to sin requires a conscious act of self-assessment; sin remains a domain the believer must cognitively refuse allegiance to through ongoing mental reckoning.

Sin is addressed as a would-be king attempting to reign in the mortal body through its passions; it seeks to co-opt bodily desires as instruments of its rule, meaning it operates through the body's appetites to assert dominion over the whole person.

Sin is the recipient to whom one may wrongly present bodily members as instruments of unrighteousness; this frames sin as a commander who deploys the body's capacities for destructive ends, and the will's decision determines whose instruments the members become.

Sin is denied dominion over the believer because they are under grace rather than law; sin's power to dominate is tied to the law's role in generating condemnation, so grace removes the legal ground on which sin's tyranny over the person stood.

The question of whether being under grace permits sinning is emphatically rejected; sinning is here a volitional act one might choose to indulge given reduced legal constraint, but grace is shown to be a stronger moral reality that forbids such license.

Sin is one of two masters a person can be enslaved to through obedience — sin leading to death, obedience to righteousness; the choice of whose slave one becomes is made at the level of the will, as the inner decision of whom to obey determines the master.

Former slavery to sin is contrasted with present heart-obedience to sound doctrine; sin's former mastery was over the whole person, but conversion involved a decisive inner turn — from the heart — that transferred allegiance away from sin's authority.

Being freed from sin and enslaved to righteousness are presented as mutually exclusive states; liberation from sin is not mere moral improvement but a change of master, relocating the person's inner allegiance from sin's dominion to righteousness entirely.

When enslaved to sin, one was entirely free from righteousness — sin and righteousness are mutually exclusive masters; this shows sin's enslavement was total, leaving no inner space for righteousness, constituting the whole person as sin's possession.

Freedom from sin produces fruit leading to sanctification and eternal life; sin's former mastery was a condition that blocked this growth entirely, showing that liberation from sin is the precondition for any progress toward holiness and ultimate life with God.

Sin pays wages — death — in stark contrast to God's free gift of eternal life; sin is here an employer whose entire compensation to the person it uses is death, making service to it a self-destructive inner allegiance with a fatal and certain outcome.
Sin is portrayed as passions operating within the body's members, aroused by the law while a person lives according to the flesh, producing fruit that leads to death.
Sin is that which the law makes knowable to the mind and conscience; without the commandment 'do not covet,' the inner reality of covetous desire as sin would have remained unrecognized.
Sin is personified as an inner force that seizes the commandment as an opportunity to produce all kinds of covetous desire within the person; apart from law, it lies dormant.
Sin is portrayed as a dormant power that springs to life within the person when the commandment arrives, with the result that the person experiences a kind of spiritual death.
Sin, using the commandment as its instrument, operates deceptively within the person, exploiting what is good to kill — revealing sin's fundamentally deceptive and lethal inner power.
Sin works death in the person through what is good, thereby exposing its own true character — it is shown to be utterly sinful, exploiting the good commandment to produce death beyond measure.
Through the commandment sin becomes visible in its full, extreme sinfulness — the law serves to magnify sin's character as exceedingly corrupt, revealing its true inner nature beyond measure.
Sin functions as a master to which the flesh-bound person is sold, depicting a state of inner enslavement where the self is under sin's ownership and control.
Sin is depicted as a power that takes up residence within the person, such that actions the person does not will are attributed to this indwelling force rather than to the self's own agency.
Sin functions as a competing law lodged in the body's members, waging war against the mind's law and taking the person captive — depicting an internal conflict between mind and flesh.
Sin operates through the flesh as a governing law, standing in sharp contrast to the law of God served by the mind — revealing a divided inner person where mind and flesh serve opposing masters.
Sin is presented as a law paired with death that once held the person captive, from which the Spirit's law in Christ Jesus has now brought liberation.
Sin is the power dwelling in sinful flesh that God condemns through Christ's incarnation, depicting sin as something embedded in fleshly human nature that required divine action to defeat.
Sin is the cause of the body's death — the physical body bears the consequence of sin — while the indwelling Spirit brings life through righteousness as the contrasting inner reality.

Corruption is depicted as a bondage that enslaves the creation itself, contrasted with the coming freedom and glory belonging to God's children — it is a condition of decay from which liberation is awaited.
Israel's trespass is presented not as final ruin but as an occasion through which salvation reached the Gentiles, turning a moral failure into an instrument of unexpected redemptive consequence.
Israel's trespass, though a moral failure, paradoxically produced riches for the world and the Gentiles, suggesting its consequences extend far beyond the act itself into global blessing.
Sins are presented as the barrier between God and his people that the new covenant removes — God's covenantal promise is specifically defined as the taking away of their sins.

Sin is defined here as action not proceeding from faith — it is rooted in the inner disposition of doubt, making the conscience and its alignment with faith the determining factor of whether an act is sinful.

To destroy is a deliberate act of desecrating what is holy — God's temple — and it carries the consequence of God's reciprocal destruction, revealing destruction as a violation with severe relational consequence.

The unrighteous are identified as those before whom believers should not bring grievances — their moral character stands in contrast to the saints, making them unsuitable arbiters of disputes within the community.
The unrighteous are those whose character excludes them from inheriting God's kingdom — the verse lists specific moral failures as evidence of a fundamental inner disposition of unrighteousness.

Sexual sin is uniquely directed against the sinner's own body — unlike other sins that are external, this transgression damages the person's own physical self, making the body itself the site of the wrong.

Every sin a person commits is described as outside the body, contrasting with sexual immorality which is a sin uniquely against the body itself — making the body the specific object of this transgression.
Marriage is explicitly declared not a sin — the verse uses the term negatively to clarify that neither marrying nor a betrothed woman marrying constitutes a moral transgression.
Marriage in response to strong passions is explicitly declared not a sin — the verse uses the negative to free the conscience of one whose inner desires make celibacy untenable.

Sinning against a brother by wounding his weak conscience is simultaneously sinning against Christ — the verse traces sin's inner impact on the conscience of the weak and its relational reach toward Christ.
Sin is presented as the fundamental problem that necessitated Christ's death — his dying 'for our sins' identifies sin as the cause requiring atoning sacrifice, in fulfilment of Scripture.

Sins are presented as a condition in which people remain if Christ has not been raised — without resurrection, faith is futile and the person is still held under sin, showing resurrection as the ground of sin's defeat.
Bad company corrupts good morals — the destruction here is of moral character through external social influence, locating the damage in the inner formation of conduct and ethics.
Ongoing sinning is linked to a state of ignorance about God — continuing in sin reflects a failure of right knowledge and spiritual alertness, and Paul names it to provoke shame and awakening.

Corruption characterizes the present body sown in death — it is the perishable nature of earthly existence, contrasted with the imperishable resurrection body, marking the physical as subject to decay.

The perishable — that which is subject to corruption — cannot inherit God's kingdom, establishing that the present corruptible bodily state is incompatible with the imperishable nature of the kingdom.
Sin is the sting of death — it is the power that gives death its lethal force — and the law in turn gives sin its power, placing sin at the centre of the chain linking law, sin, and death.
The outer self is wasting away — undergoing progressive physical deterioration — while the inner self is simultaneously renewed, contrasting outer destruction with inner restoration day by day.

Trespasses are the offences that create alienation between humanity and God; God's reconciling act in Christ consists precisely in not counting these trespasses against people, absorbing them rather than imputing them.

Sin is the condition Christ was made to be on behalf of others — he who was entirely without sin was identified with the full weight of sin so that sinners might receive God's righteousness through him.
To corrupt is listed alongside wronging and exploiting as an act Paul denies doing to anyone — it describes a deliberate moral harm done to another person's character or well-being.

Corruption here is the leading astray of the thoughts — the mind's sincere devotion to Christ is the target that the serpent-like deceiver seeks to corrupt, locating the damage in the inner faculty of the mind.

Paul questions whether humbling himself by preaching free of charge constituted a sin — the verse uses the term rhetorically to challenge any perception that self-lowering for the gospel is a moral wrong.

Sins are the condition from which Christ's self-giving delivers people — they are described as belonging to the present evil age, linking sin with a broader enslaving power from which rescue was needed.

Joining in hypocrisy is a social act of moral contagion — the rest of the Jews were drawn into Peter's insincere behaviour, and even Barnabas was led astray, showing how collective hypocrisy corrupts community integrity.

'Gentile sinners' is used as a received ethnic-religious category that Jews applied to non-Jews, reflecting a social assumption that Gentile identity was inherently marked by a sinful standing before God.

Sin appears here as the charge that would fall on Jewish believers if seeking justification in Christ means they are found to be sinners like Gentiles — Paul denies this makes Christ a promoter of sin.

Being found to be sinners while seeking justification in Christ raises the question of whether Christ enables sin — Paul's denial asserts that a sinful standing does not follow from faith-union with Christ.

A transgressor is one who rebuilds what was torn down — returning to law after abandoning it for Christ constitutes the transgression itself, making the will's reversal the act that defines one as a transgressor.

The law was added to address transgressions — human violations of divine commands — and was meant to serve this purpose only until the promised offspring came, giving transgression a historically bounded function.
Sin functions as a universal imprisoning power under which all of Scripture confines everything, so that the promise cannot be attained by human effort but only by faith in Christ.

A trespass is something a person can be caught in — suggesting a moral entanglement or surprise — and the community's response is to restore gently, with self-watchfulness against being similarly tempted.

Sowing to the flesh produces corruption as its harvest — the inner orientation toward fleshly desire yields decay and ruin as its natural end-product, contrasted with the Spirit's yield of eternal life.
Trespasses are the moral offenses from which believers need redemption; they are forgiven through Christ's blood, showing their guilt-incurring weight that required atoning sacrifice.
Sin is here a state that produces spiritual death — the person is entirely dead in it, meaning sins are not merely acts but a condition that extinguishes inner spiritual life.
