Trespasses, paired with sins, constitute the death-state of the unredeemed self — the inner person is spiritually lifeless precisely because it is immersed in these transgressions.
Trespasses are the condition of spiritual deadness from which God raises believers; being 'dead in trespasses' describes a state where the inner person has no life apart from divine intervention.
The old self is being actively corrupted through deceitful desires — corruption works from within through the faculty of desire, progressively ruining the inner person's former manner of life.
Anger itself is not here condemned as sin, but the will must act quickly to prevent it from becoming sin — the verse locates the danger in allowing anger to persist unchecked.

Slander (blasfēmia toward people) is listed among destructive inner-outward dispositions — bitterness, wrath, anger — that must be actively put away, showing it flows from a corrupted relational will.
Sins are the offenses requiring forgiveness, which redemption through Christ provides — the verse frames sin as the debt or moral deficit that makes redemption necessary.
Trespasses are the cause of spiritual death — the uncircumcised inner person is dead in them, and divine forgiveness of all trespasses is what makes alive, showing their total grip on the inner life.
Corruption here refers to the physical perishing of material things — the verse uses it to undercut the value of human rules about perishable matters, not primarily for inner-being content.
Slander is grouped with anger, wrath, and malice as something rooted in inner hostility that must be stripped off — it is an outward expression of inward corruption that believers are to renounce.

Sin is portrayed as accumulating to a full measure — the persecutors' inner rebellion against God has been filling up over time until divine wrath finally falls, showing sin as a progressively growing inner offense.
Sinners are listed among the lawless and ungodly — those whose inner moral orientation is against God's law, making them the very persons for whom the law's restraining function is designed.

Paul describes his former blasphemy as rooted in ignorant unbelief — the inner condition of unbelief drove the blasphemous behavior, yet mercy was granted because the will was not acting with full moral knowledge.

Paul identifies himself as the foremost sinner, placing the inner weight of sinfulness entirely on himself — Christ's saving mission is directed precisely toward those who bear the greatest moral guilt.
Eve's transgression resulted from being deceived — the inner faculty of perception was corrupted through deception, leading to a crossing of God's command, while Adam sinned without that deception.

Persisting in sin indicates an ongoing inner orientation toward wrongdoing that requires public rebuke — the persistence suggests the will is entrenched, and public correction aims to produce fear in others.

Hastily endorsing others can make one share in their sins — the verse warns that complicity through action implicates the inner conscience, urging self-purity by not entangling oneself in others' moral failures.
Sins differ in their visibility — some are evident before judgment, others surface only later, showing that sin can be concealed within the person for a time but will ultimately be revealed.

Slander arises from conceit and an unhealthy inner craving for controversy — the verse traces it to a diseased will and inflated ego, making it a symptom of deep inner corruption rather than an isolated act.

The mind is the specific faculty that is depraved — corruption has penetrated to the seat of understanding, so that the person is robbed of truth and distorts even godliness into a scheme for personal gain.
Blasfēmos (abusive) appears in a list of end-times character traits rooted in self-love — the abusive disposition flows from pride and self-centeredness enthroned in the inner person.
Sins burden the inner person — weak women are captured precisely because they are weighed down by accumulated sins and driven by passions, making them vulnerable to deceptive teaching.
The divisive person is described as warped and sinful, self-condemned — sin here reflects an entrenched inner distortion of character that the person's own conscience implicitly witnesses against.

Sin requires purification that only Christ can make — the verse presents sin as a defilement requiring an act of cleansing before the Son can take his exalted position, underscoring sin's separating power.

Every transgression received just retribution under the angelic message — transgression is treated as a legally culpable offense that carries a proportionate penalty, establishing the seriousness of crossing divine commands.
Sin creates a standing offense between humanity and God that requires propitiation — Christ's incarnation was necessary specifically to make that atoning provision for the people's sins.
Sin operates through deceitfulness — it works on the inner faculty of perception and the heart, hardening it gradually, making daily mutual exhortation necessary to counter its deceptive inner grip.
Those who sinned in the wilderness faced God's prolonged provocation and physical death as consequence — their sinning is the inner rebellion that drew divine wrath over forty years.

Christ was tempted yet remained without sin — sin is here the boundary that was not crossed, defining it as that which a fully human person facing real temptation can nonetheless refuse, establishing its nature as a matter of the will.

Sin creates the need for ongoing sacrificial mediation — the high priest's appointed role is to act between humanity and God by offering sacrifices for sins, showing sin as the persistent obstacle to divine access.

The human high priest shares in sin himself and must offer for his own sins before the people's — sin is a shared human condition that disqualifies even the priest from standing before God without atoning sacrifice.

God is declared not unjust — unjust here is the negated quality; God's character is the opposite of overlooking faithful love and service, affirming that divine justice is the reliable foundation of reward.
Sinners represent the morally contaminated human condition from which Christ's high priesthood is entirely separated — his holiness is defined by total inner and positional distance from sinners.

Human high priests must first sacrifice for their own sins because they share in the common sinful condition — sin inheres in the human priests themselves, requiring personal atonement before intercession for others.

Transgressions committed under the first covenant are the liability from which Christ's death redeems — they are legal-moral offenses that bound the guilty to condemnation until the new covenant's atoning death released them.

Sinning deliberately after knowing the truth engages the will directly against received knowledge — this deliberate inner choice closes off the sacrificial remedy, making the will's complicity the aggravating factor.

Sinners directed hostility against Christ — the verse highlights the active opposition of the sinful will, and Christ's endurance of it is offered as the inner resource against weariness and faintheartedness.

Enticement operates through the person's own desire — the inner faculty of desire is the hook by which temptation draws and captures the person, making the origin of temptation internal rather than external.
Showing partiality makes one a transgressor convicted by the law — the inner act of favoring persons violates the royal law, and the conscience stands condemned by the very standard it knows.

Breaking even one commandment makes a person a transgressor of the whole law — the inner will, by choosing murder even while avoiding adultery, crosses the law's boundary and incurs the full guilt of transgression.

Sinners are called to cleanse their hands while the double-minded are called to purify their hearts — the sinner's condition is externally enacted but the verse demands inner purification, linking sinfulness to a divided, unclean heart.

The sinner is one who has wandered from truth, heading toward death — turning such a person back rescues both soul and covers the accumulated sins, showing the sinner's state as an inner drift away from God.
Sinning and being punished for it earns no credit — the verse contrasts sinful action with doing good, locating the moral weight in the inner choice that precedes the suffering.

The unjust (unrighteous) are those for whom the righteous Christ died — unjust here describes the moral state of those lacking righteousness, for whose inner deficit Christ's once-for-all suffering provided the remedy.

The sinner stands in contrast to the righteous who are scarcely saved — the verse implies the sinner's moral standing leaves them in far greater peril of condemnation, with no secure path through judgment.

Corruption in the world is driven by sinful desire — it is rooted in the inner faculty of desire, and escape from it comes by partaking in divine nature, showing corruption as the inner-outward ruin desire produces.

Angels sinned and faced immediate divine judgment — the verse presents their sin as a willful act that forfeited their standing, resulting in imprisonment in darkness as direct consequence of inner moral failure.

The unrighteous are kept under ongoing punishment awaiting final judgment — their inner moral condition of injustice places them under divine custody, contrasted with the godly whom God rescues from trial.
Even mighty angels restrain themselves from uttering a blasphemous verdict against spiritual beings before God, showing that such speech exceeds what any creature's will should presume to exercise.
These false teachers, acting on mere instinct like animals and blaspheming what they do not understand, face a destruction that matches their corrupted nature — their ruin flows from within outward.
Their enticing operates from within: eyes fixed on adultery, an insatiable appetite for sin, and hearts deliberately trained in greed — inner faculties wholly bent toward luring unstable souls.
They entice through loud, empty boasting that appeals to fleshly sensual passions, targeting those barely free from error — the enticement works by exploiting weakness in the will and desires.

Corruption here is a form of inner slavery: these teachers promise freedom yet are themselves mastered by corruption, showing that whatever conquers a person's will possesses and enslaves the whole person.
Denying one's sinfulness is not merely an error of mind but a lie directed at God himself — it makes God a liar and reveals that his word has no dwelling place within the person.

Sin is treated as a real possibility for believers, but not as inevitable: the purpose of Scripture is to keep the will from sinning, and when sin does occur Christ serves as inner advocate before the Father.
Continuing to sin reveals an inner state: the one who keeps sinning has never truly seen or known Christ, whereas abiding in him structurally prevents a pattern of sin from dominating the person.

A habitual practice of sinning discloses the person's allegiance at the deepest level — it aligns the self with the devil, whose sinning is originary, making sin a mark of belonging rather than a mere act.
Divine birth transforms the inner constitution: God's seed abiding in the person makes sustained sinning structurally incompatible with who that person now is at their core.

Sin is differentiated by its direction and consequence: some sin does not sever life, and intercession can restore the sinning brother; but sin that leads to death lies beyond the scope of that intercessory remedy.

The one born of God does not keep sinning because Christ himself protects that person, so that the evil one cannot gain a foothold — sin is prevented by an external guardian reinforcing the inner new birth.
To transgress here is to go ahead and leave behind the teaching of Christ — a movement of the will away from abiding that results in losing relationship with both the Father and the Son.

Even the archangel Michael, in direct confrontation with the devil, withheld a blasphemous verdict from his own lips, deferring the pronouncement to God — showing that such speech exceeds even angelic authority.

These people are destroyed from within by the very things they act on instinctively — their ruin comes through their own base understanding, making destruction the fruit of unreflective inner corruption.
These sinners are ungodly through and through — their harsh spoken words against God are the outward expression of an inner ungodliness that pervades their deeds and their manner of acting.
The blasphemy here is a false verbal claim — those who call themselves Jews but are not slander the true community, so blasphemy operates as a lying assertion that defames what is genuinely God's.
The beast bears blasphemous names inscribed on its very heads — blasphemy is not an act here but a constitutive identity marker, the creature's nature defined by its arrogant claim against God.
The beast's mouth, given to uttering haughty and blasphemous words, exercises a permitted authority — blasphemy functions here as an empowered, willful, prolonged assault on God through speech.
Blasphemy is directed at God's name, his heavenly dwelling, and those who dwell there — it is a comprehensive verbal assault from the will, targeting God's identity, his presence, and his people.
The beast is covered in blasphemous names as its defining characteristic — blasphemy saturates its very being, making defiance of God not an occasional act but the creature's total nature.
Her crimes have accumulated to cosmic scale, heaped as high as heaven — the image of stacking iniquities suggests an inner history of willful wrongdoing that God has held in memory until judgment.

The great prostitute's corruption was active and outward-reaching — she corrupted the whole earth through her immorality, so her destruction by divine judgment is a just response to her corrupting agency.
